## PAGE 00146: Manipulating simulation information

- page_id: `227177369`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177369/Manipulating+simulation+information
- version_number: 2

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Prerequisites

1903536946

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Prerequisites

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Prerequisites

1903536951

When simulating a model, you can manipulate simulation information not only in the **Simulation** window but in diagrams as well. For example, you can change runtime values or send a trigger directly on Part shapes and Port lablels.

#### INFO: Types of diagrams

Types of diagrams

You can manipulate simulation information only in diagrams that are based on a Composite Structure Diagram.

##### Changing runtime values

You can change the runtime values of Part properties and Port labels directly in diagrams the same way you change them in the **Variables** pane of the **Simulation** window.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To display and change runtime values in diagrams, the **Show Runtime Values on Part Shapes**and/or ****Show Runtime Values on Port Labels****animation options must be enabled. For more information, see[Disabling and enabling animation options](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Displaying+simulation+information#Displayingsimulationinformation-Disablingandenablinganimationoptions).

To change runtime values on Part shapes or Port labels

1. During model simulation, select a Part shape or Port labels and click a runtime value you want to change.
2. Enter a new runtime value and press Enter. The figure below shows how to change different types of values. 33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a numeric runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing an enumeration runtime value.33%[IMAGE alt='' src='']Changing a boolean runtime value.

When you change runtime values of Value or PortProperties as described above, the simulation data is updated in real-time accordingly.

##### Sending a signal

When simulating a model, you cansend a signaldirectly from a Part shape in a diagram the same way you would send it in the **Sessions** pane of the **Simulation** window. If it is possible to send a signal, a special button is displayed on a Part shape after selecting it.

To send a signal from a Part shape

1. During model simulation, select the shape of the Part from which you want to send a signal .
2. Click the signal icon next to the part shape as displayed below. If you can send more than one signal, the icons of all available signals are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='sending_signal.png']

## PAGE 00147: Manipulating structural values

- page_id: `227178190`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178190/Manipulating+structural+values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:29:52.359+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Action languages > Action scripts APIs > Manipulating runtime objects using scripts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

You can retrieve, modify, add, or remove structural feature values using the Action Language Helper (ALH) API and action scripts in the modeling tool. ALH allows you to dynamically control object properties, and update data during a simulation. You can also retrieve tag values, which store additional metadata.

##### Getting a structural feature value

You can use script to get a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.get("****featureName")***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self.get("car.speed");

Using Groovy scripting language, you can get nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, value=car.speed

Alternatively, structural value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to get the value of the structural feature indicated above in ALH API.

```text

```

#### INFO: Information

Information

While using Rhino JavaScript, the return value of ALH.getValue() is not automatically UnBoxed to a primitive type. Users need to handle such UnBoxing by themselves, by using ALH.getValue(object, "speed").intValue() to get the value of primitive integer type.

##### Specifying a structural feature value

You can use script to specify a structural value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with the **object.set("featureName", value)***.* featureName can use dot notation for nested features. For example:

- value = self .set("speed", 10);

Using Groovy scripting language, you can set nested structural features using just dot notation. For example, car.speed=100

Alternatively, structural value can also be specified using ALH API.

```text

```

The following example shows how to specify the value of the structural feature in ALH API:

```text

```

In addition, the RuntimeObject can be omitted. The following example shows how to set a structural feature value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

##### Adding a value to an object

You can use script to add a value to an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with**object.addValue("featureName", value)**and**object.addValueAt("featureName", value, insertAt)***.*For example:

- self.addValue("p1",10); // System.p1 = [10]
- self.addValue("p1",30); // System.p1 = [10, 30]
- self.addValueAt("p1",20,2); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]

Alternatively, structural value can also be added using ALH API.

```text

```

If you have more than one value in an object and if you want to add a value at a specific position, you can use insertAt argument.

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to add values to an object, e.g.,*System.p1*, through ALH API.

```text

```

##### Removing a value of an object

You can use script to remove a value from an object. You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.removeAt("****featureName", removeAt)***.*For example:

- print(self); // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]
- self.removeAt("p1",3); // System.p1 = [10, 20]

If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

You can also use the fUML object syntax, with**object.remove("featureName", objectToRemove)**to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:

- self.remove("myPart", myPart)

object.remove("featureName", object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with [get method](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue) to get a direct reference to the fUML object.

Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.

```text

```

##### Getting a tag value

You can use script to get a tag value.You can use the fUML object syntax, with **object.getTagValue("tagName")***.* For example:

- self.getTagValue("uniform.max"); // 10

Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.

```text

```

If the RuntimeObject is omitted,**$context$**or**self**will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:

```text

```

The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g.,*max*, through ALH API. Also refer to[Value access and references by tags](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Value+access+and+references+by+tags).

```text

```

````html
ALH.addValueAt(self,"p1",20,2);        // System.p1 = [10, 20, 30]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6fa068df-ca4f-48bd-88c2-3f2affa282e7">Removing a value of an object</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>You can use script to remove a value from an object. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.removeAt(&quot;</strong><strong>featureName&quot;, removeAt)</strong></span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="89bff52b-7be2-45f5-b135-1d766858079d">For example:</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><ul><li>print(self);                                   // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20, 30]</span></li><li>self.removeAt(&quot;p1&quot;,3);               // System.p1 = <span class="error">[10, 20]</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac4ca2c0-f7f4-4ba0-ad05-b9a9ab7b226a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If feature has only one value, removeAt index 1 should be used.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="593b7398-271a-46d5-ab04-1562b476483c">Alternatively, str</ac:inline-comment-marker>uctural value can also be removed us<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1c9da7ff-c75e-40ed-adf3-d89b2fc26560">ing ALH API.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54276771-a4a3-4a39-b30b-cb3236c5e349"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValueAt(object, "featureName", removeAt)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">You can also use the fUML object syntax, with<strong><span> </span>object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, objectToRemove) </strong>to remove an exact fUML object from a feature. For example:</p><ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">self.remove(&quot;myPart&quot;, myPart)</span></li></ul><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">object.remove(&quot;featureName&quot;, object) is useful when you have direct reference to fUML object you want to remove. Remove can be used in conjunction with <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Manipulating+structural+values#Manipulatingstructuralvalues-Gettingastructuralfeaturevalue">get method</a> to get a direct reference to the fUML object.</span></p><p>Alternatively, structural value can also be removed using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60cd9055-11ec-4638-b436-5e2665690d99"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.removeValue(object, "featureName", objectToRemove)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Getting a tag value</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">You can use script to get a tag value. </span>You can use the fUML object syntax, with <strong>object.getTagValue(&quot;tagName&quot;)</strong><em>.</em> For example:</p><ul><li>self.getTagValue(&quot;uniform.max&quot;);  // 10</li></ul><p>Alternatively, tag value can also be acquired using ALH API.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a52cbee4-d7c7-4d66-84f8-d15bd36b291e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(object, "tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">If the RuntimeObject is omitted, </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span class="confluence-link">$context$</span></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> or </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">self</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> will be used as an object. The context object in the parametric is the constraint block object that owns the constraint, not the actual context of the diagram. The following example shows how to get a tag value without specifying a RuntimeObject in ALH API:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76aac9e1-855d-4bb5-b98c-b9d2f153848b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue("tagName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">The following code fragment shows how to get tag values, e.g., </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">max</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">, through ALH API. Also refer to </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Value+access+and+references+by+tags" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Value access and references by tags</a><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3cb6ad5-0daa-4d6d-af44-9d520c1f7b9a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALH.getTagValue(self, "uniform.max");	// 10]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177939 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00148: Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator

- page_id: `227177939`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177939/Manual+value+updates+using+the+Parametric+Evaluator
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-05-19T09:37:43.929+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['value-updates-with-parametric-evaluator']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

468317576

468317578

468317577

Changing values on a property of an object will cause the parametric evaluator to automatically update the other related values with the constraint defined on the object. Or, you may want to update the values with the constraint once you set all necessary values of the properties. The latter is possible by executing your model with the SimulationConfig whose fireValueChangeEvent = false (see section [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='MSI2024xR3'] for more information about the SimulationConfig and its tag definitions) and call the API provided by the parametric evaluator whenever you desire. The following figure shows an example of updating values manually with the parametric evaluator.

**Signature**: com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation.parametrics.ParametricsEngine.executeObject(Object object)

**Parameter**: Object is an object whose internal values will be updated by the parametric evaluator.

You can call the API with the MagicDraw script engine. For example, you may define it in the body of an opaque behavior. Then, use a call behavior action to call the opaque behavior somewhere in your model.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Manual Value Update with Activity in CylinderPipe.mdzip.

#### Sample model

The model used in the figures on this page is the **CylinderPipe.mdzip** sample model that comes with the modeling tool.

To open this sample, do one of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='CylinderPipe.mdzip'] **
- Find in the modeling tool <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\simulation\Parametrics\CylinderPipe.mdzip..

468317575

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177891 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00149: Many-to-one binding calculations

- page_id: `227177891`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177891/Many-to-one+binding+calculations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:00.621+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Value binding
- labels: ['many-to-one-binding-calculations']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Constraint Blocks in Magic Model Analyst support many value properties connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification without updating values back to those properties. Any value properties which are not connected to the constraint parameter will not be used as an input. The constraint parameter [*] shows a list of the connected value properties in the **Variables** pane, **Console** pane (Info), and during debugging. The result from the specification of the constraint Block is calculated from the list of value properties. For example:

```text

```

You can also use Add/Remove values with the parameter [*] in the **Variables** pane as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through many value properties.

A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Part property connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification as in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Part property.

A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Participant property of an association Block connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification. The Connector must be set type as the association Block in order to be a member of the input. Also, **{end=...}** must be specified for each Participant. Any participant properties of a Connector property will be synchronized with both the constraint parameter [*] and value properties as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Participant property.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Constraint Blocks in Magic Model Analyst support many value properties connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification without updating values back to those properties. Any value properties which are not connected to the constraint parameter will not be used as an input. The constraint parameter [*] shows a list of the connected value properties in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane, <strong>Console</strong> pane (Info), and during debugging. The result from the specification of the constraint Block is calculated from the list of value properties. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="907df08e-f0bb-4aef-a64a-ba1e1ebd20ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[inputs [*] = [2.0;3.0;4.0];
output = sum(inputs);
output = 9.0;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>You can also use Add/Remove values with the parameter [*] in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane as shown in the following figure.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Value Properties.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Many-to-one binding calculations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through many value properties.</h6><p>A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Part property connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification as in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Multiplicity.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Many-to-one binding calculations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Part property.</h6><p>A constraint Block supports many multiplicities of Participant property of an association Block connected to a constraint parameter with multiplicity [*] to perform a specification. The Connector must be set type as the association Block in order to be a member of the input. Also, <strong>{end=...}</strong> must be specified for each Participant. Any participant properties of a Connector property will be synchronized with both the constraint parameter [*] and value properties as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many-to-one Binding Supported through Multiplicities of Participant Property.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Many-to-one binding calculations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Many-to-one binding supported by constraint Blocks through multiplicities of Participant property.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177899 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00150: Mathematical equation

- page_id: `227177899`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177899/Mathematical+equation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:00.950+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Evaluating expressions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

402879988

402879990

402879989

When solving a constraint that is specified by a mathematical equation, Magic Model Analyst will find a 'target' by evaluating a 'given'. By default, a target is on the left-hand side of the equation and a given on the right-hand side of an equation.

The following figure shows an example of constraint parameters target and given in a mathematical equation.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model.

As you can see from the example shown in the preceding figure, the block **Circle** contains a constraint property typed by the constraint block **Circle** **Area**. The constraint block **Circle** **Area** has a constraint that is defined by the mathematical expression **{area =** **3.14159 * (radius** **^** **2)}**. The target and the given in the equation are the constraint parameters '**area**' and '**radius**' respectively. These constraint parameters are bound to the value properties of the block **Circle**. The constraint parameter area of e1 is bound to the value property area of the block **Circle**, and the constraint parameter radius of e1 is bound to the value property radius of the block **Circle**. So, the values of the constraint parameters and the value properties bound together are always equal.

If you simulate the block **Circle**, the parametric evaluator in Magic Model Analyst will create an object **Circle** and its internal values. It will substitute the value of radius of the block object **Circle** to the equation and calculate the value of area. Once it obtains the value of area, parametric evaluator will assign it to the area value property of the object **Circle**. If you change the value of the radius, Magic Model Analyst will automatically calculate and update the value of the area with the result from evaluating the equation.

You can also define a constraint on a UML class to constrain the values of its properties. The following figure shows a constraint on the UML class **Circle**. The constraint is defined by the same Mathematical expression as in the figure below **{area** **=** **3.14159** *** **(radius** **^** **2)}**. If you simulate the class **Circle**, the parametric evaluator will evaluate the value of radius in the equation. It will then use the value resulting from evaluating the value of radius to update the value of property **area**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class.

402879987

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating expressions' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Logical expression' space='MSI2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f229143-5138-4118-b649-9ddcb675ec8c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879988</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45810f19-0e7a-440c-8eed-6a121588c841"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879990</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db17be82-b2d5-4bda-91e8-059e715cf533"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879989</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When solving a constraint that is specified by a mathematical equation, Magic Model Analyst will find a 'target' by evaluating a 'given'. By default, a target is on the left-hand side of the equation and a given on the right-hand side of an equation. </p><p>The following figure shows an example of constraint parameters target and given in a mathematical equation. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model" ac:alt="Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model"><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationSysml.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Mathematical equation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Evaluating a Mathematical Equation in a SysML Model.</h6><p>As you can see from the example shown in the preceding figure, the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> contains a constraint property typed by the constraint block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Area</strong>. The constraint block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Area</strong> has a constraint that is defined by the mathematical expression <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">{area =</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">3.14159 * (radius</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">^</strong> <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">2)}</strong>. The target and the given in the equation are the constraint parameters '<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">area</strong>' and '<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">radius</strong>' respectively. These constraint parameters are bound to the value properties of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>. The constraint parameter area of e1 is bound to the value property area of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>, and the constraint parameter radius of e1 is bound to the value property radius of the block <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Circle</strong>. So, the values of the constraint parameters and the value properties bound together are always equal. </p><p>If you simulate the block <strong>Circle</strong>, the parametric evaluator in Magic Model Analyst will create an object <strong>Circle</strong> and its internal values. It will substitute the value of radius of the block object <strong>Circle</strong> to the equation and calculate the value of area. Once it obtains the value of area, parametric evaluator will assign it to the area value property of the object <strong>Circle</strong>. If you change the value of the radius, Magic Model Analyst will automatically calculate and update the value of the area with the result from evaluating the equation. </p><p>You can also define a constraint on a UML class to constrain the values of its properties. The following figure shows a constraint on the UML class <strong>Circle</strong>. The constraint is defined by the same Mathematical expression as in the figure below <strong>{area</strong> <strong>=</strong> <strong>3.14159</strong> *** <strong>(radius</strong> <strong>^</strong> <strong>2)}</strong>. If you simulate the class <strong>Circle</strong>, the parametric evaluator will evaluate the value of radius in the equation. It will then use the value resulting from evaluating the value of radius to update the value of property <strong>area</strong>. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class" ac:alt="Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="equationUml.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Mathematical equation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Evaluating Mathematical Equation on a UML Class.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7eae6f3c-1c04-44c0-9baf-5c7f5b40112a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">402879987</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating expressions" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Logical expression" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177757 space=MSI2024xR3 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00151: Message

- page_id: `227177757`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177757/Message
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:58.836+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Interaction simulation > Supported elements in interaction simulation
- labels: ['message']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Warning

379978608

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Warning

379978610

379978607

**On this page**

22

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Warning

379978609

According to the UML specification, a message defines a particular communication between lifelines of an Interaction. Magic Model Analyst simulates messages whose message sorts are **asynchSignal**, **synchCall**, **asynchCall**, and **reply**. You can specify a connector for the message. If the role of one connector end is the property specified by a source object of the message, Magic Model Analyst will send the message along the connector. A target object will be the object at the other end of the connector.

#### Asynchronous Signal Message (asynchSignal)

Magic Model Analyst simulates an asynchronous signal message by creating a signal object from a signal specified as a signature of the message. It sends the signal object to a target object asynchronously. The following figure shows that the bank sent the signal **maintain** to the teller using an asynchronous signal message. When the target object (the ATM object specifying the teller) received the signal, the State changed to **Maintained**.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing the simulation of an asynchronous signal message.

#### Synchronous Call Message (synchCall)

When executing a synchronous call message, Magic Model Analyst simulates a Behavior (the method of called operation of a target object) from beginning to end before it can carry out the next message. The simulation method is similar to that of the call operation Action with "isSynchronous = true".

You can substitute values for input parameters of the called operation by specifying argument values on a synchronous call message.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Simulation console showing the execution of an asynchronous call message.

The preceding figure illustrates the synchronous call messages simulation. A synchronous call message **1** shows the bank object calling the operation **retrieveAccount(accountNumber : String)** of the ledger object. The substitution value for **accountNumber** is the value of **lookupAccountNumber**, which is 0003 in this example. **lookupAccountNumber** is the property of the bank.

The simulation looked for the account object with that particular **accountNumber** and returned it to the bank object with a Reply Message **2** (2 is a Reply Message of 1). The returned account object would be specified as the value of the **buyerAccount** represented by the lifeline **buyerAccount:Account**. The bank object would then call the operation **getBalance()** of the account object with a synchronous call message **3**that caused the balance value to reply to the bank with a Reply Message **4** (4 is a Reply Message of 3). Finally, the bank called itself to print the balance value with the operation **printBalance(balance:Real)**.

#### Asynchronous Call Message (asynchCall)

The simulation of an asynchronous call message is similar to that of a synchronous call message. In this simulation, Magic Model Analyst simulates a Behavior (a method of a called operation of a target object) on a new thread. Magic Model Analyst will immediately proceed to the next message once the simulation of the Behavior starts. It will not wait until the simulation of the Behavior completes. This type of simulation is similar to that of a call operation Action with "isSynchronous = false".

#### Reply Message (Reply)

A Reply Message is a Message sent as a Reply to a Call Message (synchronous or asynchronous). If there is an argument value specified in the Reply Message, Simulation will evaluate the argument value.

#### WARNING: Warning

Warning

A Reply Message must have only one returned argument. If the Reply Message violates the rule, Simulation will print an error message in the [**Console** pane](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Console+pane).

If the argument is not the name of a property, Simulation will verify (compare) the returned value from the called operation with the argument value.

If the result of evaluating the argument is the name of a property owned by a target object, a context object, or a source object, the value returned from the operation will be defined as the value of that property. The Reply Message **2** in the preceding figure has an opaque expression whose body is **buyerAccount** as an argument value.

**buyerAccount** is the property of the **Balance Lookup** Class which is the context of a sequence diagram. Therefore, the returned account object from the operation of the ledger object **retrieveAccount(accountNumber : String)** will be set as a value of the property **buyerAccount** when the Reply Message 2 is simulated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

If the Reply Message violates the rule, Simulation will print an error message in the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Console+pane"><strong>Console</strong> pane</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If the argument is not the name of a property, Simulation will verify (compare) the returned value from the called operation with the argument value.</p><p>If the result of evaluating the argument is the name of a property owned by a target object, a context object, or a source object, the value returned from the operation will be defined as the value of that property. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227176939 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00152: Model simulation

- page_id: `227176939`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227176939/Model+simulation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:50.519+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide
- labels: ['model-simulation', 'simulating-model']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1330389784

1330389786

1330389785

Magic Model Analyst allows you to run elements simulation in a MagicDraw project. Elements that can be simulated are those that are supported by the simulation engines in Magic Model Analyst. Any number of simulation engines can be implemented as separate plugins and registered to Simulation Framework as the engines for some particular types of models.

The following table shows a list of simulation engines and their supported elements that come with Cameo SimulationToolkit

| Simulation engine | Supported elements |
| --- | --- |
| Activity engine | Activities.Activity diagrams.Classes whose classifier behavior is an Activity.InstanceSpecifications of a class whose classifier behavior is an Activity. |
| State Machine engine | State Machines.State Machine diagrams.Classes whose classifier behavior is a State Machine.InstanceSpecifications of a Class whose classifier behavior is a State Machine. |
| Interaction engine | Interactions.Sequence diagrams.Classes whose classifier behavior is an Interaction.InstanceSpecification of a Class whose classifier behavior is an Interaction. |
| Parametric engine | Blocks that contain properties that are bound to other properties (or constraint parameters) with connected binding connectors.SysML Parametric diagrams.InstanceSpecifications of a Block containing properties that are bound to other properties (or constraint parameters) with connected binding connectors. |

To run a simulation, do one of the following

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Elements simulation' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running a Simulation Configuration' space='MSI2024xR3'] including setting a target element to be simulated by the Simulation Configuration and run the model from the Simulation Configuration.

1330389783

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity simulation engine' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine simulation' space='MSI2024xR3']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177041 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00153: Model-based clock

- page_id: `227177041`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177041/Model-based+clock
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:52.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Simulation time and simulation clock
- labels: ['model-based-clock']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1350049295

1350049298

1350049296

Occasionally, you may want to have full control over the amount of simulation time to simulate your model. Magic Model Analyst allows you to use a value of any selected property to determine the simulation time. With this option, you can control the value of the simulation time with your model. The model that enables you to use the runtime value as the simulation time is called "model-based clock".

Using the model-based clock requires you to specify the property, whose value will be used as the simulation time, as the value of a **timeValue**tag definition of a Simulation Config. You can specify the unit value for the simulation time through the **timeUnit** tag definition of the Simulation Configuration. If the **timeUnit** is unspecified, the millisecond will be used by default.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Configuration with timeUnit and timeValue setting for a model-based Clock.

When you select to use a model-based clock, Magic Model Analyst will ignore the clock ratio. The following are the constraints of the model-based clock

- There should be only one object of a classifier whose property is specified as timeValue. In the preceding figure, the property time of the block Clock has been set as the timeValue of the System Execution , which is a Simulation Config for simulating the System block. There should be only one Clock object in each simulation.
- You should not use a time event to update simulation time in a clock model. Because, it obtains simulation time from the model-based clock. In addition, a time event will be fired at a specific time or duration, and simulation time cannot be updated unless it is fired. Therefore, if you use a time event, simulation time will not be updated at all.

1350049294

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Internal simulation clock' space='MSI2024xR3']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178479 space=MSI2024xR3 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00154: Monte Carlo simulation

- page_id: `227178479`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178479/Monte+Carlo+simulation
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:10.801+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > Tutorial > Analysis pattern
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

2145655572

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

2145655574

INLINE

2145655571

INLINE

**On this page**

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

2145655573

INLINE

Magic Model Analyst introduces built-in support for Monte Carlo analysis, a technique that involves using random numbers and probability to solve problems. You can manage uncertainties and estimate how random parameters affect the overall performance of the system being modeled. Please refer to the [ATTACHMENT filename='HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip'] sample model on the welcome screen as the feature demonstration with the following steps.

##### Creating a system model

1. Transform the stochastic model: D > A+B+C (random components) to the deterministic model: D-(A+B+C) > 0 .
2. Create the system model and Parts with a Block Definition diagram with required value properties, e.g., Blocks Hinge , AB , C , and D .
3. Create constraint Blocks with parameters and constraint specification according to the requirement, e.g., Clearance_equation , R1 , and R2 .
4. Create a Requirement with a satisfy Relation to the value property to keep the result for the constraint, e.g., Hinge Clearance and Unassemblable .
5. Apply a «distribution» Type to get a set of random inputs of the value properties of the Parts based on Requirements, e.g., «uniform» with max and min and «normal» with mean and standardDeviation . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Create a parametric diagram in the system to bind the value properties to the parameters of the constraint Block, e.g., Clearance equation . [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Inheriting the Hinge Analysis Block from the MonteCarloAnalysis Block

1. Create another Block Definition diagram and include an analysis context definition by dragging the MonteCarloAnalysis Block from MD Customization for SysML::analysis patterns into the newly created Block Definition diagram, e.g., AnalysisDefinition .
2. Create and inherit a new Block from the Block Definition diagram created in Step 1 as an analysis Block, e.g., Hinge Analysis , to get OutOfSpec , N , Mean , and Deviation properties. The inheritance can be done by creating a generalization Relation from the MonteCarloAnalysis Block to the system model Block (from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Monte Carlo simulation' space='MSI2024xR3'] section).
3. Create an association Relation, e.g., hinge , to make the Hinge system model block part of the analysis Block, e.g., Hinge Analysis . [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Creating a parametric diagram and binding values

1. To specify a parameter as the target of Monte Carlo analysis, create a parametric diagram in the analysis block to bind properties and constraints. The statistical value will be recorded in Mean as a result of the termination of the analysis loop, e.g., clearance .
2. Connect constraints from Requirements to Mean for the Requirement verification and to OutOfSpec for the percentage of the samples whose Mean value violates any of the attached constraints or Requirements, e.g., R1 and R2 . [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Creating a Simulation Configuration diagram and configuring other settings

1. Create a Simulation Configuration diagram, add a SimulationConfig to the newly created diagram, and set the following tags:
  - executionTarget : the analysis block, e.g., Hinge Analysis Block .
  - numberOfRuns : the number of runs, e.g., 5000 .
  - resultLocation : an Instance table, e.g., Analysis results .
  - silent : true for the optimum performance of the simulation.
  - *name* (optional): e.g., *Monte Carlo Analysis*. NoteFor a model that has Behaviors (Classifier Behavior and/or Part Property with Behaviors), see the *autoStart* tag in [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype).If *numberOfRuns* of any table (*executionTarget*) is more than 1, it will be ignored in the Monte Carlo simulation. The table, however, will be run only once.If *silent* is set to false, the simulation will run with animation and idle time for each iteration, which is not practical for the Monte Carlo simulation.
2. Drag a *[Histogram](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Histogram)* control from the Simulation toolbar to the Simulation Configuration diagram. You can use the histogram as a local user interface by setting the following tags:
  - represents : the analysis Block, e.g., Hinge Analysis .
  - value : the monitored value property, e.g., clearance .
  - dynamic : true for viewing dynamically updated statistical values ( false will open the histogram at the end of execution).
  - name (optional) : e.g., Histogram .
3. Record generated value properties of every iteration using the [CSV Export](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/CSV+Export) control by setting the following tags:
  - represents : the system model Block, e.g., Hinge .
  - value : related value properties, e.g., a.width, b.width, or clearance .
  - fileName : the exported file name, e.g., clearance.csv .
  - name (optional): e.g., clearance .
4. Drag the Histogram and CSV Export controls to the «SimulationConfig». UI and executionListeners tags will be updated with the names of the Histogram and CSV Export controls accordingly, e.g., Histogram and clearance . [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Running SimulationConfig and reviewing results

1. Run the SimulationConfig from the previous section, e.g., Monte Carlo Analysis .
2. During the simulation, the histogram will dynamically show the estimated distribution of the values of the analysis context definition at the top right, e.g., hinge.clearance, N, Mean, SD, and OutOfSpec .
3. The simulation progress bar will be shown with the number of iterations and time elapsed. You can click Cancel to terminate the simulation, and the analysis result will be saved at the terminated iteration. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. The summary result is recorded in the Instance table along with « VerificationStatus » between value property and constraint, e.g., Mean-R1 and OutOfSpec-R2 . You can also see the details of both failed and passed constraints in the tooltip when hovering the mouse over any highlighted values. [ATTACHMENT filename='analysis_results_in_instance_table.png']
5. Sampling results of value properties from applied [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Distribution Extensions' space='MSI2024xR3'] are exported to the CSV file in the same location as the project. The file can be accessed through the link, e.g., clearance.csv , in the Console pane. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68f1084c-0116-4991-ad4a-cd72c4989f6e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2145655572</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae500e7e-f6de-49c0-95dd-09494d86a590"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2145655574</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4229b8ab-1439-47bc-9ab4-bbe91e32803a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2145655571</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="688085a0-3ccf-4141-8356-244892049089"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f9cafe3-1f01-4e56-a167-202cdea3627f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2145655573</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Magic Model Analyst introduces built-in support for Monte Carlo analysis, a technique that involves using random numbers and probability to solve problems. You can manage uncertainties and estimate how random parameters affect the overall performance of the system being modeled. Please refer to the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip"><ri:page ri:space-key="CST2021xR1" ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" /></ri:attachment><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[HingeMonteCarloAnalysis]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> sample model on the welcome screen as the feature demonstration with the following steps.</p><h3>Creating a system model</h3><ol><li>Transform the stochastic model: <em>D &gt; A+B+C</em> (random components) to the deterministic model: <em>D-(A+B+C) &gt; 0</em>.</li><li>Create the system model and Parts with a Block Definition diagram with required value properties, e.g., Blocks <em>Hinge</em>,<em> AB</em>,<em> C</em>,<em> </em>and<em> D</em>.</li><li>Create constraint Blocks with parameters and constraint specification according to the requirement, e.g., <em>Clearance_equation</em>,<em> R1</em>,<em> </em>and<em> R2</em>.</li><li>Create a Requirement with a <em>satisfy</em> Relation to the value property to keep the result for the constraint, e.g., <em>Hinge Clearance </em>and<em> Unassemblable</em>.</li><li>Apply a «distribution» Type to get a set of random inputs of the value properties of the Parts based on Requirements, e.g., «uniform» with <em>max</em> and <em>min</em> and «normal» with <em>mean</em> and <em>standardDeviation</em>.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li><li>Create a parametric diagram in the system to bind the value properties to the parameters of the constraint Block, e.g., <em>Clearance equation</em>.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><h3><br />Inheriting the Hinge Analysis Block from the MonteCarloAnalysis Block</h3><ol><li>Create another Block Definition diagram and include an analysis context definition by dragging the <em>MonteCarloAnalysis</em> Block from <em>MD Customization for SysML::analysis patterns</em> into the newly created Block Definition diagram, e.g., <em>AnalysisDefinition</em>.</li><li>Create and inherit a new Block from the Block Definition diagram created in Step 1 as an analysis Block, e.g., <em>Hinge Analysis</em>, to get <em>OutOfSpec</em>,<em> N</em>,<em> Mean</em>, and <em>Deviation </em>properties. The inheritance can be done by creating a generalization Relation from the <em>MonteCarloAnalysis</em> Block to the system model Block (from the <ac:link ac:anchor="Creatingasystemmodel"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a system model]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link> section).</li><li>Create an association Relation, e.g., <em>hinge</em>, to make the <em>Hinge</em> system model block part of the analysis Block, e.g., <em>Hinge Analysis</em>.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-3.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><h3><br />Creating a parametric diagram and binding values</h3><ol><li>To specify a parameter as the target of Monte Carlo analysis, create a parametric diagram in the analysis block to bind properties and constraints. The statistical value will be recorded in <em>Mean</em> as a result of the termination of the analysis loop, e.g., <em>clearance</em>.</li><li>Connect constraints from Requirements to <em>Mean</em> for the Requirement verification and to <em>OutOfSpec</em> for the percentage of the samples whose <em>Mean</em> value violates any of the attached constraints or Requirements, e.g., <em>R1</em> and <em>R2</em>.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-4.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><h3><br />Creating a Simulation Configuration diagram and configuring other settings</h3><ol><li>Create a Simulation Configuration diagram, add a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> to the newly created diagram, and set the following tags:<br /><ul><li><em>executionTarget</em>: the analysis block, e.g., <em>Hinge Analysis Block</em>.</li><li><em>numberOfRuns</em>: the number of runs, e.g., <em>5000</em>.</li><li><em>resultLocation</em>: an Instance table, e.g., <em>Analysis results</em>.</li><li><em>silent</em>: <strong>true</strong> for the optimum performance of the simulation.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><em>name</em> (optional): e.g., <em>Monte Carlo Analysis</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7a639dec-c9c7-42a7-961a-488c112eba6a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">For a model that has Behaviors (Classifier Behavior and/or Part Property with Behaviors),</span> see the <em>autoStart</em> tag in <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a>.</li><li>If <em>numberOfRuns</em> of any table (<em>executionTarget</em>) is more than 1, it will be ignored in the Monte Carlo simulation. The table, however, will be run only once.</li><li>If <em>silent</em> is set to false, the simulation will run with animation and idle time for each iteration, which is not practical for the Monte Carlo simulation.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li>Drag a <em><em><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Histogram">Histogram</a></em></em> control from the <strong>Simulation</strong> toolbar to the Simulation Configuration diagram. You can use the histogram as a local user interface by setting the following tags:<br /><ul><li><em>represents</em>: the analysis Block, e.g., <em>Hinge Analysis</em>.</li><li><em>value</em>: the monitored value property, e.g., <em>clearance</em>.</li><li><em>dynamic</em>: <strong>true</strong> for viewing dynamically updated statistical values (<strong>false</strong> will open the histogram at the end of execution).</li><li><em>name</em> (optional)<em>:</em> e.g., <em>Histogram</em>.</li></ul></li><li>Record generated value properties of every iteration using the <em><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/CSV+Export">CSV Export</a></em> control by setting the following tags:<br /><ul><li><em>represents</em>: the system model Block, e.g., <em>Hinge</em>.</li><li><em>value</em>: related value properties, e.g., <em>a.width, b.width,</em> or <em>clearance</em>.</li><li><em>fileName</em>: the exported file name, e.g., <em>clearance.csv</em>.</li><li><em>name</em> (optional): e.g.,<em> clearance</em>.</li></ul></li><li>Drag the <em>Histogram</em> and <em>CSV Export</em> controls to the «SimulationConfig». <em>UI</em> and <em>executionListeners</em> tags will be updated with the names of the <em>Histogram</em> and <em>CSV Export</em> controls accordingly, e.g., <em>Histogram</em> and <em>clearance</em>.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-5.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol><h3><br />Running SimulationConfig and reviewing results</h3><ol><li>Run the SimulationConfig from the previous section, e.g.,<em> Monte Carlo Analysis</em>.</li><li>During the simulation, the histogram will dynamically show the estimated distribution of the values of the analysis context definition at the top right, e.g., <em>hinge.clearance, N, Mean, SD,</em> and <em>OutOfSpec</em>.</li><li>The simulation progress bar will be shown with the number of iterations and time elapsed. You can click <strong>Cancel</strong> to terminate the simulation, and the analysis result will be saved at the terminated iteration.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-6.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li>The summary result is recorded in the Instance table along with «<a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/Recording+verification+status+of+runtime+values">VerificationStatus</a>» between value property and constraint, e.g., <em>Mean-R1</em> and <em>OutOfSpec-R2</em>. You can also see the details of both failed and passed constraints in the tooltip when hovering the mouse over any highlighted values.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="analysis_results_in_instance_table.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Sampling results of value properties from applied <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Distribution Extensions" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[distributions stereotypes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are exported to the CSV file in the same location as the project. The file can be accessed through the link, e.g., <em><u>clearance.csv</u></em>, in the <strong>Console</strong> pane.<br /><br /><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="7204-8.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Monte Carlo simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178074 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00155: Nested Connector end

- page_id: `227178074`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178074/Nested+Connector+end
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:04.346+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Supported SysML elements
- labels: ['nested-connector-end']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If the ends of a Connector that connects properties are stereotyped with «NestedConnectorEnd», Magic Model Analyst will use the information from the propertyPath of the nested Connector end to find the right objects that specify the properties at both ends of the Connector. Therefore, you can send a Signal along the Connector to a deep nesting object. You can also use the binding Connector to bind objects that specify the properties at different nesting levels.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If the ends of a Connector that connects properties are stereotyped with «NestedConnectorEnd», Magic Model Analyst will use the information from the propertyPath of the nested Connector end to find the right objects that specify the properties at both ends of the Connector. Therefore, you can send a Signal along the Connector to a deep nesting object. You can also use the binding Connector to bind objects that specify the properties at different nesting levels. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177216 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00156: Nested property selection for configurations

- page_id: `227177216`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177216/Nested+property+selection+for+configurations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:53.278+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['nested-properties', 'selecting-nested-properties-for-configurations']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Magic Model Analyst, from version 18.4, includes support for nested property selection for configurations, such as Time series charts, Timelines, Sequence diagram generator, and CSV export.

The Specification window for the aforementioned items will have the following two properties

- **Represents** Specifies the Classifier represented by the Configuration.
- Value The Value property takes nested property values.

The **Select Nested Properties** dialog will open if you click the button beside the Value property from the Specification window.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Select Nested Properties dialog.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

Before the **Value** property can be specified, the **Represents** property has to be assigned a value first.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Magic Model Analyst, from version 18.4, includes support for nested property selection for configurations, such as Time series charts, Timelines</span><span style="color: black;">, Sequence diagram generator, and CSV export.</span></p><p class="Textbody"><span style="color: black;">The Specification window for the aforementioned items will have the following two properties<br /></span></p><ul><li class="Textbody"><span style="color: black;"><strong>Represents<br /></strong></span><span>Specifies the Classifier represented by the Configuration.</span></li><li class="Textbody"><strong style="color: black;line-height: 1.42857;">Value</strong><span style="color: black;"> <br />The Value property takes nested property values.</span></li></ul><p class="Textbody"><span style="color: black;">The <strong>Select Nested Properties</strong> dialog will open if you click the button beside the Value property from the Specification window.  <br /> </span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: black;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Select Nested Properties Dialog" ac:alt="Select Nested Properties Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="nested-properties-dialog-a.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested property selection for configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><span style="color: rgb(128,128,128);">Select Nested Properties dialog.</span></span></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e351f37c-e660-4f44-af9a-ecb849208f4d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Before the <strong>Value</strong> property can be specified, the <strong>Represents</strong> property has to be assigned a value first.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177172 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00157: Nested UI Configuration stereotype

- page_id: `227177172`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177172/Nested+UI+Configuration+stereotype
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:53.114+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['nested-ui-configuration-stereotype']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1422162079

1422162082

1422162080

Magic Model Analyst provides a «NestedUIConfig» stereotype to create a complex UI mockup, which consists of multiple UI Configs. This stereotype contains two tag definitions: (i) feature and (ii) config. The feature tag is mandatory. It specifies which Property (part) of the context it will represent. The Config tag specifies which existing UI configuration will be used and displayed as the UI of the system part, which is represented by the Property specified in the feature tag. A Nested UI Configuration can also show images of UI that is defined as the tagged value of Config tag definition, such as Panel, activeImage, and Time series chart.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Images of Nested User Interface Configuration.

You can use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to either [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing parts of a simulation context' space='MSI2024xR3'] (This part can nest components that represent the nested properties) or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing parts using existing UI Configurations' space='MSI2024xR3']. The NestedUIConfig stereotype can be applied with the UI Panel or UI Group Box. When it is applied and its tag definitions are set, it can be represented as a part of its owner component.

1422162078

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7d268bbb-7cad-42d0-b1a8-52eeb434b94f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1422162079</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81f732fa-cd41-4044-929e-ddef9586e286"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1422162082</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60aa8b9d-2aa8-4737-9f0e-b51d8ce12797"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1422162080</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Magic Model Analyst provides a «NestedUIConfig» stereotype to create a complex UI mockup, which consists of multiple UI Configs. This stereotype contains two tag definitions: (i) feature and (ii) config. The feature tag is mandatory. It specifies which Property (part) of the context it will represent. The Config tag specifies which existing UI configuration will be used and displayed as the UI of the system part, which is represented by the Property specified in the feature tag. A Nested UI Configuration can also show images of UI that is defined as the tagged value of Config tag definition, such as Panel, activeImage, and Time series chart.</p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Images of Nested User Interface Configuration" ac:alt="Images of Nested User Interface Configuration"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedConfigShowingImages2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested UI Configuration stereotype" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Images of Nested User Interface Configuration.</h6><p>You can use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to either <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts of a simulation context" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[represent a part of a simulation context]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (This part can nest components that represent the nested properties) or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[representing a part using existing UI Configurations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The NestedUIConfig stereotype can be applied with the UI Panel or UI Group Box. When it is applied and its tag definitions are set, it can be represented as a part of its owner component.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e3d3f885-d7ea-4cc0-8051-eab8d2d06aa6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1422162078</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b644387e-6f6b-4afa-aef2-28ab32dc5ebb" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177887 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00158: Object binding

- page_id: `227177887`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177887/Object+binding
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-10-02T10:45:29.435+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Value binding
- labels: ['object-binding']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If a binding connector connects properties that is typed by either a class or a block, the runtime value that specifies each property will be the referent of the same block object. Since you can use only a SysML binding connector to bind objects, the SysML plugin is required.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Object Binding with a SysML Binding Connector.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If a binding connector connects properties that is typed by either a class or a block, the runtime value that specifies each property will be the referent of the same block object. Since you can use only a SysML binding connector to bind objects, the SysML plugin is required. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Object Binding with a SysML Binding Connector" ac:alt="Object Binding with a SysML Binding Connector"><ri:attachment ri:filename="objectBinding.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Object binding" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Object Binding with a SysML Binding Connector.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177344 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00159: Opening a diagram of an executing behavior

- page_id: `227177344`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177344/Opening+a+diagram+of+an+executing+behavior
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:54.041+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Animation
- labels: ['opening-executing-behavior-diagram', 'diagram-of-executing-behavior']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When you simulate a model, the **Sessions** pane will open to display simulation of each element in the model. During the simulation, if you ever need to see what diagram a Behavior being simulated has, you can double-click the session in the **Sessions** pane of the **Simulation** window. The following figure shows the **Sessions** pane when Magic Model Analyst is executing the **Blackjack** sample (*Blackjack.mdzip*). If you double-click the Behaviors outlined in red, a new tab will open to display the corresponding diagram.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Sessions tab of the Simulation window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When you simulate a model, the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane will open to display simulation of each element in the model. During the simulation, if you ever need to see what diagram a Behavior being simulated has, you can double-click the session in the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane of the <strong>Simulation</strong> window. The following figure shows the <strong>Sessions</strong> pane when Magic Model Analyst is executing the <strong>Blackjack</strong> sample (<em>Blackjack.mdzip</em>). If you double-click the Behaviors outlined in red, a new tab will open to display the corresponding diagram. </p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="The Sessions Tab of the Simulation Window" ac:alt="The Sessions Tab of the Simulation Window"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sessionsTab.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Opening a diagram of an executing behavior" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Sessions tab of the Simulation window.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177955 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00160: Operators

- page_id: `227177955`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177955/Operators
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-09-03T13:23:19.779+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator > Built-in Math
- labels: ['operators']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### NOTE: Important

Important

• **x**and **y** represent numerical values or variables. 
• **m**, **n**, and **p**represent integer values or variables. 
• **a**and **b** represent boolean values or variables. 
• **U**and **V**represent matrices of numerical values. 
• **A**and **B**represent matrices of boolean values.

#### Arithmetic operators

| Operator | Operator name | Syntax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| + | Addition | x+yU + V (U and V are m x n matrices) |
| - | Subtraction | x-yU + V (U and V are m x n matrices) |
| * | Multiplication | x*yU*V (U is an m x n matrix and V is an n x p matrix) |
| / | Division | x/y |
| % | Modulus | m%nU + V (U and V are m x n matrices of integer values).This operator operates element-wise on matrices. |
| ! | Factorial | m! |
| ^ | Power | x^y |
| \ | Left division | x\y is equivalent to (1/x) * yU \ V (U and V are m x n matrices) is equivalent to (1/U) * V |
| .* | Element-wise multiplication | U .* V (U and V are m x n matrices) |
| ./ | Element-wise division | U ./ V (U and V are m x n matrices) |
| .\ | Element-wise left division | U .\ V (U and V are m x n matrices) is equivalent to (1/U) .* V |
| .^ | Element-wise power | U .^ V (U and V are m x n matrices) |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

An Element-wise operator performs an operation on each pair of Elements, which is in the same location, of the operand matrices.

#### Assignment operators

| Operator | Operator name | Syntax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| = | Assignment | x=ya=bU=V |

#### Comparison operators

| Operator | Operator name | Syntax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| > | Greater | x>yU>V |
| < | Less | x<yU<V |
| >= | Greater or Equal | x>=yU>=V |
| <= | Less of Equal | x<=yU<=V |
| == | Equality | x==ya==bU==V |
| != | Inequality | x!=ya!=bU!=V |

All comparison operators operate Element-wise on matrices in the example as follows

A = [1; 2; 3] 
B = [3; 2; 1]

Then 
A>B is [false; false; true];

#### Boolean operators

| Operator | Operator Name | Syntax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ! NOTnot | NOT | !ANOT Anot A |
| &ANDand | AND | A&BA AND BA and B |
| \|ORor | OR | A\|BA OR BA or B |
| ^XORxor | XOR (exclusive OR) | A^BA XOR BA xor B |

#### NOTE: Important

Important

All boolean operators operate element-wise on matrices in the example as follows

A = [true; true; false; false]; 
B = [true; false; true; false];

Then 
A&B is [true; false; false; false];
