<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177060 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00178: Representing enumeration values

- page_id: `227177060`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177060/Representing+enumeration+values
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:52.308+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-enumeration-values', 'enumeration-value-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent an enumeration value

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and specify the enumeration, whose values will be represented by an ActiveImage of the ImageSwitcher, as the tagged value of the represent tag definition of the ImageSwitcher element.
2. Create an Active image for each enumeration literal owned by the enumeration.
3. Specify an image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage, and set the enumeration literal that will be represented by the ActiveImage as the activeElement tag definition.

You can use*SmallTestSamples.mdzip* as your example. This file is a sample project of Magic Model Analyst. It is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder. In this example, an ImageSwitcher was created to represent a VerdictKind enumeration. An ActiveImage was created for each enumeration literal of the VerdictKind as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image switcher and Active Image representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals.

If a property is typed by an enumeration, you can use the ImageSwitcher and the ActiveImage to represent it with a UI frame mockup of the Classifier that owns the property. Once you drag the property to the panel owned by the UI frame mockup, Magic Model Analyst will prompt you to select either an ImageSwitcher or a group of radio buttons to represent the value. If you select the ImageSwitcher, the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the default value of the property, will open in the panel or in the group box to which the property has been dragged.

The figure below shows the User Interface Modeling diagram of the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. It contains a UI frame that represents the **Test Result** dialog, which is the Classifier owning the property typed by the VerdictKind enumeration. The property is **result:VerdictKind,**whose default value is **pass**. Now, the ActiveImage that represents the value **pass** will be shown in the panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Modeling diagram containing a UI frame with Image Switcher.

When the UI mockup opens in the MagicDraw window during model simulation, the image shown in the panel containing the ImageSwitcher will be the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the runtime value of the property.

You can simulate the Test Result dialog in the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample project. You can also change a result value by selecting one of the radio buttons that represents the value: pass, fail, inconclusive, or error. Now the image that corresponds to the value will appear.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Mockup whowing the property's value typed by enumeration.

You can view more information on reusing ImageSwitcher in the UI mockup in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space='MSI2024xR3'].

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Setting the **show empty image for unspecified value** option (true/ false) on the «ImageSwitcher» will change the image on the web UI through ImageSwitcher to be the same as the image that represents the simulation UI.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The generated HTML button on the toolbar of the UI diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### ImageSwitcher on the web UI representing the enumeration value that corresponds to the simulation UI.

Another more comprehensive and realistic sample is available in Magic Model Analyst is *Blackjack.mdzip*. This sample contains a Blackjack game simulation in which Players can only stand or hit, and the Dealer can stand on 17 or higher. Once you run the Run Blackjack Simulation Config, the Blackjack game will begin by showing a Blackjack game table UI (see the following blackjack figure).

Note that this sample also demonstrates the **Nested UI Configuration** feature (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nested UI Configuration stereotype' space='MSI2024xR3'] for more information). As the Dealer/Player has 7 possible card slots, according to the **Nested** **UI** **Configuration** feature, the Dealer/Player thus has 7 corresponding Value Properties, e.g., 'Card1', 'Card2', 'Card3', and so on.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI showing Blackjack Table in the Blackjack.mdzip sample.

The image showing the card slots demonstrates the application of ImageSwitcher and enumeration. Each card slot is actually a UI Panel having the **NestedUIConfig** stereotype applied in which the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **config** tag is set to the **Card Image Switcher** ImageSwitcher, and the **NestedUIConfig****'s** **feature** tag is set to the corresponding Value Property, e.g., 'Card1'. The **Card Image Switcher**ImageSwitcher is set to represent the **Card** **Value** enumeration. The **Card** **Image** **Switcher** ImageSwitcher contains 53 ActiveImages, each of which is the corresponding Enumeration Literal of the **Card Image** enumeration. Note the **Card Value** enumeration also contains 53 Enumeration Literals.

- '0' Enumeration Literal represents a blank image; a card slot without any card.
- '1' - '52' Enumeration Literals represent the images of 52 distinct cards.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Image Switcher containing active image of 52 cards and blank image.

When the value of one of the 7 Value Properties changes, the card slot image will change accordingly. See the Player's cards in the figure to better understand how it works as follows

- The value of the Player's 'Card1' Value Property is '49' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '49' ActiveImage. The '49' ActiveImage represents the ten of spades.
- The value of the Player's 'Card2' Value Property is '28' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '28' ActiveImage. The '28' ActiveImage represents the two of hearts.
- The value of the Player's 'Card3' Value Property is '0' Enumeration Literal, which represents a blank card slot. Therefore, no card is open next to the two of hearts card.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI and Variables panel showing the value of 7 value properties and the displayed image.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing object states' space='MSI2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h4>To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent an enumeration value </h4><hr /><ol><li>Create an ImageSwitcher element, and specify the enumeration, whose values will be represented by an ActiveImage of the ImageSwitcher, as the tagged value of the <strong>represent</strong> tag definition of the ImageSwitcher element.</li><li>Create an Active image for each enumeration literal owned by the enumeration.</li><li>Specify an image that will be the <strong>image</strong> attribute of each created ActiveImage, and set the enumeration literal that will be represented by the ActiveImage as the <strong>activeElement</strong> tag definition. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You can use<em> SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> as your example. This file is a sample project of Magic Model Analyst. It is located in the <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation</em> folder. In this example, an ImageSwitcher was created to represent a VerdictKind enumeration. An ActiveImage was created for each enumeration literal of the VerdictKind as follows <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Image Switcher and Active Image Representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals" ac:alt="Image Switcher and Active Image Representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals"><ri:attachment ri:filename="imageswitcher_for_enum.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Image switcher and Active Image representing Enumeration and Enumeration Literals.</h6><p>If a property is typed by an enumeration, you can use the ImageSwitcher and the ActiveImage to represent it with a UI frame mockup of the Classifier that owns the property. Once you drag the property to the panel owned by the UI frame mockup, Magic Model Analyst will prompt you to select either an ImageSwitcher or a group of radio buttons to represent the value. If you select the ImageSwitcher, the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the default value of the property, will open in the panel or in the group box to which the property has been dragged. </p><p>The figure below shows the User Interface Modeling diagram of the <em>SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> sample project. It contains a UI frame that represents the <strong>Test Result</strong> dialog, which is the Classifier owning the property typed by the VerdictKind enumeration. The property is <strong>result:VerdictKind, </strong>whose default value is <strong>pass</strong>. Now, the ActiveImage that represents the value <strong>pass</strong> will be shown in the panel. </p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Modeling Diagram Containing a UI Frame with Image Switcher" ac:alt="UI Modeling Diagram Containing a UI Frame with Image Switcher"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI frame in UI modeling diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI Modeling diagram containing a UI frame with Image Switcher.</h6><p>When the UI mockup opens in the MagicDraw window during model simulation, the image shown in the panel containing the ImageSwitcher will be the ActiveImage that represents the enumeration literal, which is the runtime value of the property. </p><p>You can simulate the Test Result dialog in the <em>SmallTestSamples.mdzip</em> sample project. You can also change a result value by selecting one of the radio buttons that represents the value: pass, fail, inconclusive, or error. Now the image that corresponds to the value will appear. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Mockup Showing the Property's Value typed by Enumeration" ac:alt="UI Mockup Showing the Property's Value typed by Enumeration" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UI mockup with property value.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI Mockup whowing the property's value typed by enumeration.</h6><p>You can view more information on reusing ImageSwitcher in the UI mockup in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested UI Configuration stereotype" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link>. </p><p class="Textbody">The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Setting the <strong>show empty image for unspecified value</strong> option (true/ false) on the «ImageSwitcher» will change the image on the web UI through ImageSwitcher to be the same as the image that represents the simulation UI.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The generated HTML button on the toolbar of the UI diagram.</h6><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="imageSwitcher_enum.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">ImageSwitcher on the web UI representing the enumeration value that corresponds to the simulation UI.</h6><p>Another more comprehensive and realistic sample is available in Magic Model Analyst is <em>Blackjack.mdzip</em>. This sample contains a Blackjack game simulation in which Players can only stand or hit, and the Dealer can stand on 17 or higher. Once you run the Run Blackjack Simulation Config, the Blackjack game will begin by showing a Blackjack game table UI (see the following blackjack figure). </p><p>Note that this sample also demonstrates the <strong>Nested UI Configuration</strong> feature (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Nested UI Configuration stereotype" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link> for more information). As the Dealer/Player has 7 possible card slots, according to the <strong>Nested</strong> <strong>UI</strong> <strong>Configuration</strong> feature, the Dealer/Player thus has 7 corresponding Value Properties, e.g., 'Card1', 'Card2', 'Card3', and so on. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI Showing Blackjack Table in Blackjack.mdzip" ac:alt="UI Showing Blackjack Table in Blackjack.mdzip"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UI showing Blackjack Table in the Blackjack.mdzip sample.</h6><p>The image showing the card slots demonstrates the application of ImageSwitcher and enumeration. Each card slot is actually a UI Panel having the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong> stereotype applied in which the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong><strong>'s</strong> <strong>config</strong> tag is set to the <strong>Card Image Switcher</strong> ImageSwitcher, and the <strong>NestedUIConfig</strong><strong>'s</strong> <strong>feature</strong> tag is set to the corresponding Value Property, e.g., 'Card1'. The <strong>Card Image Switcher </strong>ImageSwitcher is set to represent the <strong>Card</strong> <strong>Value</strong> enumeration. The <strong>Card</strong> <strong>Image</strong> <strong>Switcher</strong> ImageSwitcher contains 53 ActiveImages, each of which is the corresponding Enumeration Literal of the <strong>Card Image</strong> enumeration. Note the <strong>Card Value</strong> enumeration also contains 53 Enumeration Literals. </p><ul><li>'0' Enumeration Literal represents a blank image; a card slot without any card.</li><li>'1' - '52' Enumeration Literals represent the images of 52 distinct cards. <br /><br /></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Image Switcher Containing Active Image of 52 Cards and Blank Image" ac:alt="Image Switcher Containing Active Image of 52 Cards and Blank Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack2.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">Image Switcher containing active image of 52 cards and blank image.</h6><p>When the value of one of the 7 Value Properties changes, the card slot image will change accordingly. See the Player's cards in the figure to better understand how it works as follows</p><ul><li>The value of the Player's 'Card1' Value Property is '49' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '49' ActiveImage. The '49' ActiveImage represents the ten of spades.</li><li>The value of the Player's 'Card2' Value Property is '28' Enumeration Literal, which is the activeElement of the '28' ActiveImage. The '28' ActiveImage represents the two of hearts.</li><li>The value of the Player's 'Card3' Value Property is '0' Enumeration Literal, which represents a blank card slot. Therefore, no card is open next to the two of hearts card. <br /><br /></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="UI and Variable Panel Showing the Value of 7 Value Properties and the Displayed Image" ac:alt="UI and Variable Panel Showing the Value of 7 Value Properties and the Displayed Image"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Blackjack3.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 30.0px;">UI and Variables panel showing the value of 7 value properties and the displayed image.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing object states" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177053 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00179: Representing object states

- page_id: `227177053`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177053/Representing+object+states
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-13T07:43:49.414+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage
- labels: ['representing-object-states', 'state-representation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.

To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state

1. Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its represent tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.
2. Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.
3. Specify the image that will be the image attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the activeElement tag definition.

The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the *FlashingLight.mdzip* sample)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.

The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.

###### Sample model

The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='FlashingLight.mdzip'] .
- Find it in the modeling tool <*install_root*>\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Representing enumeration values' space='MSI2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can use an ActiveImage to represent each state of a runtime object whose Classifier is represented by the ImageSwitcher that owns the ActiveImage. The <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample is used throughout this page. You can open the sample by downloading it from the attached link.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use the ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent a state</p><hr /><ol><li>Create an ImageSwitcher element, and set its <strong>represent</strong> tag definition with a Classifier whose state will be represented by an active image owned by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Create an ActiveImage in the ImageSwitcher for each State of the Classifier represented by the ImageSwitcher.</li><li>Specify the image that will be the <strong>image</strong> attribute of each created ActiveImage and set the State, which will be represented by the ActiveImage, as the tagged value of the <strong>activeElement</strong> tag definition. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The following figure illustrates an example of how to use ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage to represent the States of a runtime object (see the <em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em> sample)</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-1.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing object states" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Using ImageSwitcher in the FlashingLight.mdzip sample with internal simulation clock.</h6><p>The image that appears on the web UI through ImageSwitcher is the same image that represents the current State of simulation UI. If the image on the simulation UI is changed, the image on the Web UI will change accordingly and vice versa. Either image is able to accept a signal by clicking the mouse.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="6742-2.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing object states" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Setting startWebServer to true in the Simulation Config to show the web UI.</h6><h4>Sample model</h4><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the FlashingLight.mdzip sample model that comes with MagicDraw. To open this sample, do either of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Download <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="FlashingLight.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing object states" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.</li><li>Find it in the modeling tool <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;\samples\simulation\FlashingLight.mdzip.</em></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="ImageSwitcher and ActiveImage" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing enumeration values" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177174 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00180: Representing parts of a simulation context

- page_id: `227177174`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177174/Representing+parts+of+a+simulation+context
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-13T07:34:22.185+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Nested UI Configuration stereotype
- labels: ['displaying-parts-of-simulation-context', 'showing-parts-of-simulation-context', 'simulation-context']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A UI Panel or UI Group Box to which a NestedUIConfig stereotype is applied and a feature tag set can represent some parts and nest other components. One of the samples that shows such purpose is the *SmallTestSamples.mdzip* sample, which is located in the *<*install_root*>\samples\simulation* folder.

The following System Class diagram shows the structures of Class system and Class monitor

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Structures of Class system and Class monitor.

On the same Class diagram, there are Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Instance Specifications of Class system and Class monitor.

The User Interface Modeling diagram displays the UI configuration that will be used in the simulation as follows

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UI Configuration of Class system.

The following figure displays the UI configuration and the Specification of the UI Panel named panel1, which represents the monitor1 Property, as a part of Class system

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
UI Configuration and its Specification.

When the **test_nested_panels** SimulationConfig is run, the UI mockup will be displayed. The following figure exhibits the UI Panels and UI Group Boxes that represent the parts (Properties) of the Class system and in-depth nested parts as well 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Simulation Variables panel and Runtime UI Mockup.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177180 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00181: Representing parts using existing UI Configurations

- page_id: `227177180`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177180/Representing+parts+using+existing+UI+Configurations
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-03-13T07:47:39.965+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Nested UI Configuration stereotype
- labels: ['displaying-parts-using-ui-configurations', 'ui-configurations']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to show a part using existing UI Configurations. Any UI Panels or UI Group Boxes that will be used for this purpose must be applied with the NestedUIConfig stereotype and must also be set for both the feature and Config tags. The benefit of this purpose is that you can reuse existing UI Configurations to illustrate any parts of contexts that have the same Class represented. Thus, you do not need to create a new UI Configuration whenever you want to represent another part that has the same Class represented.

The FlashLight sample will be used to show how to model a part using an existing UI Configuration. The following steps show you how to create a UI Mockup that represents the entire system parts, which uses only one UI Frame

1. Open the FlashLight sample, which is located in the *<*install_root*>/samples/simulation* directory (*FlashingLight.mdzip*). The following System Definition Class diagram shows the definition of the FlashLight system. By default, when executing and running this sample, you will see the Button and Light are shown in different Frames. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The following figure demonstrates the runtime User Interface Mockup that represents the Class Button and Class Light [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Right-click the Containment tree and select New Diagram > Other Diagrams > User Interface Modeling Diagram to create a User Interface Modeling diagram.
3. Create a UI Frame on the UI Modeling diagram by dragging the Class system to the newly created UI Frame as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Create two UI panels in the UI Frame.
5. Drag the Light property to one of the UI panels to apply the NestedUIConfig stereotype to the UI Panel and set the **Light** property to the feature tag of that UI panel as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Drag an existing UI Configuration named LampBulb to the UI panel that represents the Light property. This will set the dragged UI Configuration to the Config tag of that UI panel as follows [IMAGE alt='' src=''] After assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI panel representing the Light Property, you will see the tagged value Specification of the UI panel that represents the Light property in the Specification of the UI Panel dialog as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Drag the Button property to another UI panel. Now the UI panel represents the Button property as part of Class system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Drag an existing UI Configuration named PowerButton to the UI panel that represents the Button property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. Create a new Simulation Configuration element on any Simulation Configuration diagrams; set the simulationTarget tag to Class system and set the UI tag to the UI Frame that represents the Class system. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Simulate and run the newly created Simulation Configuration. The UI Mockup will appear as illustrated in the following figure

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also use a NestedUIConfig stereotype to show a part using existing UI Configurations. Any UI Panels or UI Group Boxes that will be used for this purpose must be applied with the NestedUIConfig stereotype and must also be set for both the feature and Config tags. The benefit of this purpose is that you can reuse existing UI Configurations to illustrate any parts of contexts that have the same Class represented. Thus, you do not need to create a new UI Configuration whenever you want to represent another part that has the same Class represented. </p><p>The FlashLight sample will be used to show how to model a part using an existing UI Configuration. The following steps show you how to create a UI Mockup that represents the entire system parts, which uses only one UI Frame <span style="color: rgb(125,133,154);"> <br /></span></p><ol><li><p>Open the FlashLight sample, which is located in the <em>&lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em>&gt;/samples/simulation</em> directory (<em>FlashingLight.mdzip</em>). The following System Definition Class diagram shows the definition of the FlashLight system. By default, when executing and running this sample, you will see the Button and Light are shown in different Frames. </p><h6><ac:image ac:title="System Definition Class Diagram of the FlashLight Sample" ac:alt="System Definition Class Diagram of the FlashLight Sample"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_6.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>The following figure demonstrates the runtime User Interface Mockup that represents the Class Button and Class Light</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The Runtime UI Mockup of Class Button and Light" ac:alt="The Runtime UI Mockup of Class Button and Light"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Right-click the Containment tree and select <strong>New</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Other</strong> <strong>Diagrams</strong> &gt; <strong>User</strong> <strong>Interface </strong><strong>Modeling</strong> <strong>Diagram</strong> to create a User Interface Modeling diagram.</li><li><p>Create a UI Frame on the UI Modeling diagram by dragging the Class system to the newly created UI Frame as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Frame Representing Class System" ac:alt="The UI Frame Representing Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UIFrameRepresentingClassSystem.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create two UI panels in the UI Frame.</li><li><p>Drag the Light property to one of the UI panels to apply the NestedUIConfig stereotype to the UI Panel and set the <strong>Light</strong> property to the feature tag of that UI panel as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Panel Representing the Light Property as a Part of Class System" ac:alt="The UI Panel Representing the Light Property as a Part of Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_9.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Drag an existing UI Configuration named LampBulb to the UI panel that represents the Light property. This will set the dragged UI Configuration to the Config tag of that UI panel as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI Panel Representing the Light Property" ac:alt="Assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI Panel Representing the Light Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_10.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p>After assigning the LampBulb UI Configuration to the UI panel representing the Light Property, you will see the tagged value Specification of the UI panel that represents the Light property in the Specification of the UI Panel dialog as follows</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Tagged Value Specification of the UI Panel Representing the Light Property" ac:alt="Tagged Value Specification of the UI Panel Representing the Light Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-panel.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Drag the Button property to another UI panel. Now t<span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">he UI panel represents the Button property as part of Class system.</span></p><h6><ac:image ac:title="The UI Panel Representing the Button PropertyAs Part of Class System" ac:alt="The UI Panel Representing the Button PropertyAs Part of Class System"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_12.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Drag an existing UI Configuration named PowerButton to the UI panel that represents the Button property.</p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_13.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li><p>Create a new Simulation Configuration element on any Simulation Configuration diagrams; set the simulationTarget tag to Class system and set the UI tag to the UI Frame that represents the Class system.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Execution Configuration Elements and Its Specificatons" ac:alt="Execution Configuration Elements and Its Specificatons"><ri:attachment ri:filename="flashlight-config.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Simulate and run the newly created Simulation Configuration. The UI Mockup will appear as illustrated in the following figure</li></ol><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The UI Mockup Representing Class System and Its Parts" ac:alt="The UI Mockup Representing Class System and Its Parts"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUI_15.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Representing parts using existing UI Configurations" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178088 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00182: Requirement and test case traceability

- page_id: `227178088`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178088/Requirement+and+test+case+traceability
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:05.153+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['test-case-traceability']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

During a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst lists all constraints and requirements or test cases that are related in the model in the Console pane. When the simulation fails to satisfy or fulfill some constraints, it will cause the model simulation to fail and errors to occur. You can see the error messages and the links in the **Console** pane and **Variables** pane, to navigate to the requirement(s) that cause the test case to fail.

A tooltip message of a failed test case in the **Variables** pane is in the example as follows.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Tracing to Failed Constraints.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>During a model simulation, Magic Model Analyst lists all constraints and requirements or test cases that are related in the model in the Console pane. When the simulation fails to satisfy or fulfill some constraints, it will cause the model simulation to fail and errors to occur. You can see the error messages and the links in the <strong>Console</strong> pane and <strong>Variables</strong> pane, to navigate to the requirement(s) that cause the test case to fail. </p><p>A tooltip message of a failed test case in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane is in the example as follows. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Tracing to Failed Constraints" ac:alt="Tracing to Failed Constraints"><ri:attachment ri:filename="link_toReq.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirement and test case traceability" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Tracing to Failed Constraints.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178083 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00183: Requirement refined by a constraint block

- page_id: `227178083`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178083/Requirement+refined+by+a+constraint+block
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:05.027+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['requirement-refined-by-constraint-block']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you use a constraint block, which refines a SysML requirement, to type a constraint property of a block, you can select the object of the constraint property to navigate into a SysML requirement that is related to that object in the Containment tree. 
 
To navigate to a SysML requirement related to a constraint property in the Containment tree

1. Right click a constraint property object in the Variables pane and select Go To (see the following figure).
2. Select the name of a requirement that appears on the sub menu. Magic Model Analyst will show you the SysML requirement that is related to the constraint property specified by the selected object in the Containment tree. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property.

The preceding figure shows the block **Circle** whose constraint property typed by the constraint block **Maximum****Area**, which refines the requirement **Maximum****Area**. It also shows you that if you right-click the object that specifies the constraint property when Magic Model Analyst is executing the block **Circle**, and select the **Go****To** context menu, you will see the requirement **Maximum****Area**. Selecting the requirement on the menu will highlight that requirement in the Containment tree.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you use a constraint block, which refines a SysML requirement, to type a constraint property of a block, you can select the object of the constraint property to navigate into a SysML requirement that is related to that object in the Containment tree. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To navigate to a SysML requirement related to a constraint property in the Containment tree </p><hr /><ol><li>Right click a constraint property object in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane and select <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong> (see the following figure).</li><li><p>Select the name of a requirement that appears on the sub menu. Magic Model Analyst will show you the SysML requirement that is related to the constraint property specified by the selected object in the Containment tree. </p><h6><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property" ac:alt="Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_sysmlRequirement.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirement refined by a constraint block" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Navigating into a SysML Requirement related to a Constraint Property.</h6></li></ol><p>The preceding figure shows the block <strong>Circle</strong> whose constraint property typed by the constraint block <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>, which refines the requirement <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>. It also shows you that if you right-click the object that specifies the constraint property when Magic Model Analyst is executing the block <strong>Circle</strong>, and select the <strong>Go </strong><strong>To</strong> context menu, you will see the requirement <strong>Maximum </strong><strong>Area</strong>. Selecting the requirement on the menu will highlight that requirement in the Containment tree. </p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178085 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00184: Requirement satisfied by a property

- page_id: `227178085`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178085/Requirement+satisfied+by+a+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-06-30T11:57:58.575+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models > Requirements traceability from the Variables pane
- labels: ['requirement-satisfied-by-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also select a value that specifies a property in the **Variables** pane to navigate into a SysML Requirement, which is satisfied by the property, in the Containment tree. When you right-click the value and select the **Go** **To** context menu, you will see a specific Requirement satisfied by the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Navigating into a Requirement satisfied by a Property.

Simulation Toolkit supports contextualized satisfy relationship that value property from inner nested structure is connected to Requirements. For example, the *constraint on value* Requirement must be satisfied by the *length of the width of Rectangle* object only in this context (not the *length of the height of Rectangle*, *length of Square*, or *length of Line* objects) through the **Source Context** and **Source Property Path** tags as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Contextualized satisfy relationship through the Source Context and Source Property Path tags.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also select a value that specifies a property in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane to navigate into a SysML Requirement, which is satisfied by the property, in the Containment tree. When you right-click the value and select the <strong>Go</strong> <strong>To</strong> context menu, you will see a specific Requirement satisfied by the property. </p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Navigating into a Requirement Satisfied by a Property" ac:alt="Navigating into a Requirement Satisfied by a Property"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_reqtypedbyProperty.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirement satisfied by a property" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Navigating into a Requirement satisfied by a Property.</h6><p>Simulation Toolkit supports contextualized satisfy relationship that value property from inner nested structure is connected to Requirements. For example, the <em>constraint on value</em> Requirement must be satisfied by the <em>length of the width of Rectangle</em> object only in this context (not the <em>length of the height of Rectangle</em>, <em>length of Square</em>, or <em>length of Line</em> objects) through the <strong>Source Context</strong> and <strong>Source Property Path</strong> tags as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Contextualized Satisfy Relationship.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Requirement satisfied by a property" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Contextualized satisfy relationship through the Source Context and Source Property Path tags.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178082 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00185: Requirements traceability from the Variables pane

- page_id: `227178082`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178082/Requirements+traceability+from+the+Variables+pane
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:04.933+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models
- labels: ['requirement-traceability']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When you model with SysML, you can create a relationship between a SysML requirement and any element in your model. Magic Model Analyst allows you to navigate from the runtime value or from the object, to the related SysML requirement during the model simulation.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>When you model with SysML, you can create a relationship between a SysML requirement and any element in your model. Magic Model Analyst allows you to navigate from the runtime value or from the object, to the related SysML requirement during the model simulation. </p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178106 space=MSI2024xR3 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00186: Results Player

- page_id: `227178106`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178106/Results+Player
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-07-24T08:18:00.411+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation of SysML models
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

3

The Results Player displays numerical values from a CSV file directly in composite structure diagrams, such as Internal Block Diagram, Parametric, etc. This allows you to review the simulation results, specific time steps, and display results of an external simulation (for example, a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SSP Export - Technology Preview' space=''].

You can view the active States for the selected parts in the Results Player when you load a CSV file. To learn more about how to export active States for the parts, refer the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='CSV export' space=''] page.

You can view an image of the active state next to the state label in the Results Player. Each state machine supports only one active state; Composite and orthogonal states are not supported. To learn more, refer to the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying simulation information' space=''] page.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites for displaying Active State Images

Prerequisites for displaying Active State Images

- The StateMachine must be set as Classifier Behavior.
- Only a single state must be active at a time.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Results Player when a file is loaded.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Active State Image displayed when the file is loaded in the Results Player.

##### Understanding the UI components of the Results Player

The following table describes the UI components of the Results Player:

| Icon | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Load/Unload File | Selects a CSV export file to load/Deselects the CSV export file. |
|  | Beginning | Stops the play and jumps to the first step. |
|  | Step Backward | Jumps to the previous step. |
|  | Play | Starts automatic playback of time values in real-time. |
|  | Step Forward | Jumps to the next step. |
|  | End | Stops the play and jumps to the last step. |
|  | Playback speed | Controls the playback speed. |
|  | Slider | Displays the current position and allows you to manually scroll through the values. |
|  | Time indicator | Shows the current step and allows you to manually enter the desired time value. |
|  | Time Series Chart | Generates the Time Series Chart. |
|  | Export to Instance | Generates the Instance. |
|  | Verify Requirements | Verifies the requirements present in the loaded CSV file.The green dot indicates that verification is successful and no requirement has failed.The red dot indicates that some of the requirements have failed. |
|  | The green dot indicates that verification is successful and no requirement has failed. |  |
|  | The red dot indicates that some of the requirements have failed. |  |

##### Launching the Results Player

You can launch the Results Player both through the Containment tree and the composite structure diagram.

To launch the Results Player

- In the Containment tree, right-click the composite structure diagram and select Tools> Results Player . [ATTACHMENT filename='Result Player_Containement Tree.png']
- In the composite structure diagram, right-click anywhere and select Tools>Results Player. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Loading CSV export file

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- The CSV file must be comma-delimited.
- The first column must contain time values. Time units can be specified in parentheses; otherwise, seconds are used. Supported time units are ns, µs, ms, s, min, h, d, mo, y.
- Other column headers must have property paths specified in dot notation, such as engine.valveTrain.speed. U nrecognized columns are ignored.

You must load the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='CSV export' space=''] file to be able to use the Results Player. You can load the CSV export file either manually or automatically after the simulation terminates.

To load the CSV export file manually

1. Click on the Load File command in the Results Player. [ATTACHMENT filename='Manually loading file.png']
2. In the Open dialog, select the CSV file and click Open . [ATTACHMENT filename='Open Dialog.png']

- You can drag and drop the CSV file from the file system on the Result Player. You can also drag and drop the CSV file from the Containment tree on the Result Player and the composite diagram.
- SysML properties are mapped to CSV file headers depending on the diagram where the file is loaded. For example, if the file has information on a System, then it must be loaded in a diagram displaying the System structure. It cannot be loaded in other level diagrams (such as Controller or Water Tank). Results can also be seen in the nested structure diagrams.

To load a CSV export file automatically when a simulation terminates:

1. In the Specification of CSV export dialog, set the Load in Player property to true. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Generating a Time Series Chart

You can generate a Time Series Chart with help of Results Player.

To generate a Time Series Chart

1. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon, found on the Result Player.
2. From the contextual menu, select the required values and click**OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] While selecting values in the dialog, you can add up to ten values at a time. If there are more than ten values, click **Select All**to****select the initial ten values in the dialog.

The generated Time Series Chart is displayed for the selected values. You can also change/update the values by clicking the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon in the Time Series Chart. You can generate multiple charts with different values at the same time. When a CSV file is unloaded, charts are deactivated, but remain open for a reference. Additionally, you can load a new CSV export file and generate new Time Series Charts. A black vertical line indicates the current time step and you can change the time step by clicking on the chart. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Time Series Chart

When you export the CSV file automatically and specify the Time Series chart in the simulation configuration, the Time Series chart is generated automatically with the same values as the original configuration.

##### Exporting the current step to an Instance Specification

To export the current step to an Instance Specification

1. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon present on the Result Player.
2. From the Selecting Owner or Instance dialog, select a Package to generate a new instance, or select an existing instance to update its values. Click**OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- The created instance is highlighted in blue in the Containment tree.
- If an instance has several classifiers set, then you will only work with one that matches your target.

##### Verifying the Requirements

You can verify requirements in the Results Player when you export the CSV file.You can verify requirements that are specified with the help of the Requirement patterns; however, the requirements specified using a constraint block are not supported. To learn more about how to use the requirement patterns, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Requirement patterns glossary' space='CRMP2024xR3'] glossary.

To verify the requirements

1. Load the CSV export file. To learn more, refer to section.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='Verify Requirements_Symbol.png'] icon present on the Results Player. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The Verify Requirements command is disabled ifno requirements in the context are compatible

Once you click the Verify Requirements icon, the icon is updated with a red or green dot in it. The [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon indicates that the verification is successful and no requirements have failed. The [IMAGE alt='' src=''] icon indicates that some of the requirements have failed.In the Block diagram, the label of a verified value is displayed green if it is passing at the current time step, and it is displayed red if it is failing at the current time step. A tooltip along with the red label is displayed for the failing value. Also, the failing regions are highlighted in red on the Slider for the failedrequirements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A red label with tooltip, a green label and failing region on the Slider displayed in the Block diagram and on the Results Player respectively.

Requirement verification also has a drop down menu with extra commands to increase the usability.

| Sr. No. | Icon | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 |  | The default action is same as the main command. |
| 2 |  | The command exports all mapped data to a new CSV file and adds a column with requirements verification results. For example, if the verified requirement is WaterLevel, then the requirements verification results column is added after the WaterLevel column. The name of the added column includes ID and name of the requirement. |
| 3 |  | Clears the verified results. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95239092-f83b-4135-8a93-6a47941983b3"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The Results Player displays numerical values from a CSV file directly in composite structure diagrams, such as Internal Block Diagram, Parametric, etc. This allows you to review the simulation results, specific time steps, and display results of an external simulation (for example, a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SSP Export - Technology Preview" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SSP export)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="76db2f20-7255-4689-8876-d6d6d73a3239">You</ac:inline-comment-marker> can view the active States for the selected parts in the Results Player when you load a CSV file. data-mce-resize="false"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="26"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement_Passed.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The green dot indicates that verification is successful and no requirement has failed.</span></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="26"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement_Failed.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The red dot indicates that some of the requirements have failed.</span></td></tr></tbody></table></td></tr></tbody></table><h3><span>Launching the Results Player</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;">You can launch the Results Player both through the Containment tree and the composite structure diagram.</p><p style="text-align: left;">To launch the Results Player</p><hr /><ul><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the composite structure diagram and select <strong>Tools&gt; Results Player</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Result Player_Containement Tree.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the composite structure diagram, right-click anywhere and select <strong>Tools&gt;Results Player.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Result Player_IBD Diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /></strong></li></ul><h3>Loading CSV export file</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="173c2af6-3112-4f4a-a319-fc135045d30c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The CSV file must be comma-delimited.</li><li>The first column must contain time values. Time units can be specified in parentheses; otherwise, seconds are used. Supported time units are <strong>ns, µs, ms, s, min, h, d, mo, y.</strong><span> </span></li><li>Other column headers must have property paths specified in dot notation, such as engine.valveTrain.speed. U<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">nrecognized columns are ignored.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>You must load the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="CSV export" /></ac:link> file to be able to use the Results Player. You can load the CSV export file either manually or <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">automatically after the simulation terminates.</span></p><p>To load the CSV export file manually</p><hr /><ol><li>Click on the <strong>Load File </strong>command in the Results Player.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Manually loading file.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the Open dialog, select the CSV file and click<strong> Open</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Open Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="17628b39-821e-453c-8020-8be512d82af6"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You can drag and drop the CSV file from the file system on the Result Player. You can also drag and drop the CSV file from the Containment tree on the Result Player and the composite diagram.</li><li>SysML properties are mapped to CSV file headers depending on the diagram where the file is loaded. For example, if the file has information on a System, then it must be loaded in a diagram displaying the System structure. It cannot be loaded in other level diagrams (such as Controller or Water Tank). Results can also be seen in the nested structure diagrams.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To load a CSV export file automatically when a simulation terminates:</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the <span>Specification of CSV export dialog, set </span>the Load in Player property to <em>true.</em><span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification of CSV Export.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol><h3><span>Generating a Time Series Chart</span></h3><p><span>You can generate a Time Series Chart with help of Results Player.</span></p><p><span>To generate a Time Series Chart</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="24"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Time Series Chart_Symbol.png" /></ac:image> icon, found on the Result Player.</span></li><li><span>From the contextual menu, select the required values an<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a06f3b9a-ff55-469b-bd65-cd7c84aa1e9c">d click </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a06f3b9a-ff55-469b-bd65-cd7c84aa1e9c">OK</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a06f3b9a-ff55-469b-bd65-cd7c84aa1e9c">.</ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Value dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /></span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7cd1ad4e-3669-4069-a6ce-93d418ef1601"><ac:rich-text-body><p>While selecting values in the dialog, you can add <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">up to ten values at a time. If there are more than ten values, click <strong>Select All </strong>to<strong> </strong>select the initial ten values in the dialog.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><span>The generated Time Series Chart is displayed for the selected values. You can also change/update the values by clicking the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="24"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Filter Icon.png" /></ac:image> icon in the Time Series Chart. You can generate multiple charts with different values at the same time. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">When a CSV file is unloaded, charts are deactivated, but remain open for a reference</span>. Additionally, you can load a new CSV export file and generate new Time Series Charts. A black vertical line indicates the current time step and you can change the time step by clicking on the chart. <br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Time Series Chart.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>Time Series Chart</span></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cedd618c-cb1f-423a-854f-750eedf4fd04"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When you export the CSV file automatically and specify the Time Series chart in the simulation configuration, the Time Series chart is generated automatically with the same values as the original configuration.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Exporting the current step to an Instance Specification</h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span>To export the current step to an Instance Specification</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="24"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Instance_Icon.png" /></ac:image> icon present on the Result Player.</span></li><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">From the Selecting Owner or Instance dialog, select a Package to generate a new instance, or select an existing instance to update its values. Click </span><strong style="text-align: left;">OK</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">.</span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Selecting Instance dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1581d921-f20a-4bee-b1e4-579c2ef33ef4"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span>The created instance is highlighted in blue in the Containment tree.</span></li><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">If an instance has several classifiers set, then you will only work with one that matches your target.</span></span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Verifying the Requirements</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You can verify requirements in the Results Player when you export the CSV file.<span> </span></span>You can verify requirements that are specified with the help of the Requirement patterns; however, the requirements specified using a constraint block are not supported. To learn more about how to use the requirement patterns, refer to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CRMP2024xR3" ri:content-title="Using Requirement patterns glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Requirement Patterns]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> glossary.<br /><br /></p><p>To verify the requirements</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Load the CSV export file. To learn more, refer to </span><ac:link ac:anchor="Loading CSV export file" /> section.</li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Click </span><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="28"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Verify Requirements_Symbol.png" /></ac:image><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">icon present on the Results Player.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Click Verify requirements.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a64f970-5a13-457b-baad-8c80faddce5a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Verify Requirements command is disabled if<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="834c99da-bfb3-4ffb-b172-53221941532d"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> no requirements in the context are compatible</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Once you click the Verify Requirements icon, the icon is updated with a <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">red or green dot in it. The <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="26"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement_Passed.png" /></ac:image> icon indicates that the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">verification is successful and no requirements have failed. The <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="26"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement_Failed.png" /></ac:image> icon indicates that some of the requirements have failed. </span></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">In the Block diagram, the label of a verified value is displayed green if it is passing at the current time step, and it is displayed red if it is failing at the current time step. A tooltip along with the red label is displayed for the failing value. Also, the <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">failing regions are highlighted in red on the Slider for the failed</span></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> requirements.<br /><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="404" ac:width="978"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Both labels with Tooltip.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A red label with tooltip, a green label and failing region on the Slider displayed in the Block diagram and on the Results Player respectively.</h6><p style="text-align: left;">Requirement verification also has a drop down menu with extra commands to increase the usability.</p><table class="wrapped" data-mce-resize="false"><colgroup class=""><col class="" /><col class="" /><col class="" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Sr. No.</p></div></th><th style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Icon</p></div></th><th style="vertical-align: middle;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Description</span></th></tr><tr><td style="text-align: center;vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>1</p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Verify Requirements_Symbol_Dropdown.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The default action is same as the main command</span>.</span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td style="text-align: center;vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>2</p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Export Results to CSV.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The command exports all mapped data to a new CSV file and adds a column with requirements verification results. </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">For example, if the verified requirement is WaterLevel, then the r<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">equirements verification results column is added </span>after the  WaterLevel column. The name of the added column includes <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">ID and </span>name <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">of the requirement.</span></span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td style="text-align: center;vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>3</p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Clear Verification.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td><td style="vertical-align: middle;">Clears the verified results.</td></tr></tbody></table></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177193 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00187: Reusable UI Mockup

- page_id: `227177193`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177193/Reusable+UI+Mockup
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:53.202+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling
- labels: ['reusing-ui-mockup', 'ui-mockup']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1424462686

1424462688

1424462687

Magic Model Analyst improves the flexibility in user interface modeling. You can reuse UI components by typing the property using the UI component. The object, which is the value of the property, represents a mock-up of the UI component. Once the object is created, the mock-up UI will be created and shown in the MagicDraw window. Whenever the object is deleted, the mock-up UI will be deleted as well. It is not necessary to add the UI component to the UI tag definition of the Simulation Config stereotype.

To create and reuse a UI component

1. Create a UI Frame on a UI Modeling element diagram.
2. Drag the created UI Frame from the Containment browser to the UI Frame symbol in the UI Modeling diagram to create a UI prototype as follows [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Create a property in the block. Whenever this property is initialized, the UI Frame simulation will run.
4. Use the UI Frame as a type for the created property. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can use a UI Frame to create other properties and define it as the type of the properties. A UI Mockup will start and will be deleted with an object that is the value of the property typed by a UI component (including but not limited to the property in Step 3. You can show or hide a UI Mockup by using the context menu of the instance of the property.

To hide a UI mockup

1. Right-click an instance of a property and click Hide Mock-up in the Variable panel.
2. Click the Close button.

To show a hidden UI mockup

- Right-click an instance of a property and click Show Mock-up .

You can also show or hide the other UI components, for example, time series chart and ImageSwitcher.

1424462685

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model' space='MSI2024xR3']

You can reuse UI components by typing the property using the UI component. The object, which is the value of the property, represents a mock-up of the UI component. Once the object is created, the mock-up UI will be created and shown in the MagicDraw window. Whenever the object is deleted, the mock-up UI will be deleted as well. It is not necessary to add the UI component to the UI tag definition of the Simulation Config stereotype. </p><p>To create and reuse a UI component</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a UI Frame on a UI Modeling element diagram.</li><li><p>Drag the created UI Frame from the Containment browser to the UI Frame symbol in the UI Modeling diagram to create a UI prototype as follows</p><h6><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="dragUIframe.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="Reusable UI Mockup" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li><li>Create a property in the block. Whenever this property is initialized, the UI Frame simulation will run.</li><li><p>Use the UI Frame as a type for the created property.</p><h6><ac:image ac:title="Using the UI Frame as the Property's Type" ac:alt="Using the UI Frame as the Property's Type"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using UI frame as a property type.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Reusable UI Mockup" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6></li></ol><p>You can use a UI Frame to create other properties and define it as the type of the properties. A UI Mockup will start and will be deleted with an object that is the value of the property typed by a UI component (including but not limited to the property in Step 3. You can show or hide a UI Mockup by using the context menu of the instance of the property. </p><p>To hide a UI mockup</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click an instance of a property and click <strong>Hide</strong> <strong>Mock-up</strong> in the <strong>Variable</strong> panel.</li><li>Click the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Close</strong> button.</li></ol><p>To show a hidden UI mockup</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click an instance of a property and click <strong>Show</strong> <strong>Mock-up</strong>.</li></ul><p> <br class="atl-forced-newline" />You can also show or hide the other UI components, for example, time series chart and ImageSwitcher. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b712532-36d6-44f7-945b-4617b6146e75"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1424462685</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating User Interface mockups for the stopwatch model" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
