<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178474 space=MSI2024xR3 version=4 -->
## PAGE 00188: Rollup Pattern simulation

- page_id: `227178474`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178474/Rollup+Pattern+simulation
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:36:10.643+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > Tutorial > Analysis pattern
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

643911130

643911151

643911129

**On this page**

33

643911141

Magic Model Analyst supports rollup calculations of total mass, cost, power, and another system dimension, based on individual values of all the parts in the model. Please refer to [Rollup Pattern Wizard](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Rollup+Pattern+Wizard), [applying Rollup Pattern Blocks](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks), and **LaptopCostAnalysis** and **SpacecraftMassRollup** built-in samples for more details.

##### Simulation with a built-in rollup pattern

The **LaptopCostAnalysis** sample applies built-in **CostRollUpPattern**. The sample uses the **Cost Analysis** Block with the **Laptop** context Block as the Part. Optionally, you can add any constraint (*maxCost*) and Requirement (*Total cost*) and use a Parametric diagram to connect them as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Cost Analysis Block containing the Laptop context Block applied with built-in CostRollUpPattern as the Part.

You can also either run the simulation through the **Cost Analysis** Block directly or use a [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype) with *executionTarget* *= the Cost Analysis Block*. The result will be shown in the **totalCost** context Block, e.g., 220 (80+50+90) as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The totalCost context Block as the result of Laptop cost analysis applied with CostRollUpPattern.

##### Simulation with a custom rollup pattern

The **SpacecraftMassRollup** sample applies a custom rollup pattern through the same [applying Rollup Pattern Block](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks) like the built-in one but requires selecting a Pattern Block for the custom rollup pattern, e.g., **Component**. The **Component****Pattern Block**uses value type properties binding with 5 constraint properties in the Parametric diagram as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Parametric diagram of the Component custom rollup pattern as a Pattern Block.

Optionally, you can add any constraint (*less*) and Requirement (*Estimated mass*)*,* and then run the *spacecraft mass analysis* [SimulationConfig](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype) with the *executionTarget* as an instance of the spacecraft to run. The result of evaluation, according to the *me < ma* rule, will be highlighted at *me : mass[kilogram]* for each component shown on the right side in the Block Definition diagram as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The evaluation result (me : mass[kilogram]) of the SpacecraftMassRollup sample applied with the Component custom rollup pattern.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="03d7910c-8a95-4a81-b386-6f289aaac8cf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">643911130</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="48df4958-3a82-4297-88cc-9d46d7490230"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">643911151</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3a8eb74d-7d38-4338-95f6-c891e8dd8b20"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">643911129</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38008b6e-5e35-433a-8a12-6ba5dd5f94f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a2e6c8ba-116d-41a1-ba46-1048b0997d33"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">643911141</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Magic Model Analyst supports rollup calculations of total mass, cost, power, and another system dimension, based on individual values of all the parts in the model. Please refer to <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Rollup+Pattern+Wizard">Rollup Pattern Wizard</a>, <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks">applying Rollup Pattern Blocks</a>, and <strong>LaptopCostAnalysis</strong> and <strong>SpacecraftMassRollup</strong> built-in samples for more details.</p><h3>Simulation with a built-in rollup pattern</h3><p>The <strong>LaptopCostAnalysis</strong> sample applies built-in <strong>CostRollUpPattern</strong>. The sample uses the <strong>Cost Analysis</strong> Block with the <strong>Laptop</strong> context Block as the Part. Optionally, you can add any constraint (<em>maxCost</em>) and Requirement (<em>Total cost</em>) and use a Parametric diagram to connect them as shown in the figure below.</p><p align="center"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cost Analysis Block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Rollup Pattern simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Cost Analysis Block containing the Laptop context Block applied with built-in CostRollUpPattern as the Part.</h6><p>You can also either run the simulation through the <strong>Cost Analysis</strong> Block directly or use a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> with <em>executionTarget</em> <em>= the Cost Analysis Block</em>. The result will be shown in the <strong>totalCost</strong> context Block, e.g., 220 (80+50+90) as shown in the figure below.</p><p align="center"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="TotalCost context Block.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Rollup Pattern simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The totalCost context Block as the result of Laptop cost analysis applied with CostRollUpPattern.</h6><h3>Simulation with a custom rollup pattern</h3><p>The <strong>SpacecraftMassRollup</strong> sample applies a custom rollup pattern through the same <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/SYSMLP2024xR3/Applying+Rollup+Pattern+Blocks">applying Rollup Pattern Block</a> like the built-in one but requires selecting a Pattern Block for the custom rollup pattern, e.g., <strong>Component</strong>. The <strong>Component</strong><em> </em>Pattern Block<em> </em>uses value type properties binding with 5 constraint properties in the Parametric diagram as shown below.</p><p align="center"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Parametric Diagram of the Component.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Rollup Pattern simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Parametric diagram of the Component custom rollup pattern as a Pattern Block.</h6><p>Optionally, you can add any constraint (<em>less</em>) and Requirement (<em>Estimated mass</em>)<em>,</em> and then run the <em>spacecraft mass analysis</em> <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/SimulationConfig+stereotype">SimulationConfig</a> with the <em>executionTarget</em> as an instance of the spacecraft to run. The result of evaluation, according to the <em>me &lt; ma</em> rule, will be highlighted at <em>me : mass[kilogram]</em> for each component shown on the right side in the Block Definition diagram as shown below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="900"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Evaluation Result of the SpacecraftMassRollup Sample.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Rollup Pattern simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The evaluation result (me : mass[kilogram]) of the SpacecraftMassRollup sample applied with the Component custom rollup pattern.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177702 space=MSI2024xR3 version=3 -->
## PAGE 00189: Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin

- page_id: `227177702`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177702/Running+a+Call+Action+simulation+without+a+target+pin
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:58.152+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Activity simulation
- labels: ['call-action-simulation']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

375306651

375306653

375306652

When Magic Model Analyst simulates a CallOperationAction that does not have a target pin, it will select a runtime object, which is the context of the Activity, as a target. For example, a System Class, which owns a Sub-System Class (see the second figure below), is the context of the SystemBehavior Activity shown in the first figure. The CallOperationAction will call the SayHello operation using the Activity context, which in this case is the System Class.

If an Action belongs to an Activity partition, and the Activity partition represents part of a Classifier, which is a context of that Activity, Magic Model Analyst will select the runtime object specified by the part as the target of the Action.

However, Magic Model Analyst will direct the target object to the callOperationAction only if an element represented by the Activity partition is a property (or an inherited property) of the Classifier, which owns the Activity and if there is only one runtime object specified by the property represented by the Activity partition. For example, if the CallOperationAction that calls the Print operation is in the Activity partition, which represents the SubSystem property of the System Class, this will result in a call to invoke the Print operation in the SubSystem property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Activity System Behavior. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Class diagram describing system Class structure.

375306650

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Call Operation Action' space='MD2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior' space='MSI2024xR3']

For example, a System Class, which owns a Sub-System Class (see the second figure below), is the context of the SystemBehavior Activity shown in the first figure. The CallOperationAction will call the SayHello operation using the Activity context, which in this case is the System Class. </p><p>If an Action belongs to an Activity partition, and the Activity partition represents part of a Classifier, which is a context of that Activity, Magic Model Analyst will select the runtime object specified by the part as the target of the Action. </p><p>However, Magic Model Analyst will direct the target object to the callOperationAction only if an element represented by the Activity partition is a property (or an inherited property) of the Classifier, which owns the Activity and if there is only one runtime object specified by the property represented by the Activity partition. For example, if the CallOperationAction that calls the Print operation is in the Activity partition, which represents the SubSystem property of the System Class, this will result in a call to invoke the Print operation in the SubSystem property. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Activity System Behavior" ac:alt="Activity System Behavior"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ActivitySysBehavior.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Activity System Behavior.<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Class Diagram Describing System Class Structure" ac:alt="Class Diagram Describing System Class Structure"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ClassDescribes Sysstructure.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a Call Action simulation without a target pin" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Class diagram describing system Class structure.</h6><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1c82bf58-e46c-4845-9379-f42f4b4e8a7b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">375306650</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" ri:content-title="Call Operation Action" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity Partition execution and allocated Behavior" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227176991 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00190: Running a Simulation Configuration

- page_id: `227176991`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227176991/Running+a+Simulation+Configuration
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-10-25T08:03:13.012+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Model simulation
- labels: ['simulating-simulation-configuration']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1333564395

1333564398

1333564396

Cameo Simulation Toolkit provides a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='MSI2024xR3'] that you can use to run a model with specified simulation properties, e.g. include Charts, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reusable UI Mockup' space='MSI2024xR3'], specify time properties, and more.

To run a Simulation Configuration, do one of the following

- In the main toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='run_button.png'] next to the Simulation Configuration name. [ATTACHMENT filename='running_simulation_configuration.png']
- In the diagram toolbar, click a small arrow next to [ATTACHMENT filename='run_button.png'] and select the Simulation Configuration you want to run . If you want to execute a specific element displayed in a diagram, select the element shape before selecting the Simulation Configuration. [ATTACHMENT filename='simconfig_from_diagram_toolbar.png']
- Right-click an element in the Containment tree or right-click the element shape in a diagram and select Simulation , then select the Simulation Configuration you want to run. [ATTACHMENT filename='simconfig_from_shortcut_menu.png']
- In the Simulation Configuration Diagram , right-click a Simulation Configuration shape and select Simulation > Run .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

To terminate a Simulation Configuration, do one of the following

- In the main toolbar, click the Terminate button next to the Simulation Configuration name. [ATTACHMENT filename='terminating_simulationConfig_from_main_toolbar.png']
- Click the Terminate button in the Simulation pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='terminating_simulationConfig_from_simulation_pane.png']

1333564393

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation Configuration and UI modeling' space='MSI2024xR3']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177603 space=MSI2024xR3 version=4 -->
## PAGE 00191: Running a State Machine simulation

- page_id: `227177603`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177603/Running+a+State+Machine+simulation
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:35:57.320+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation
- labels: ['executing-state-machines']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

123795415

123795417

123795416

Magic Model Analyst will perform a State Machine simulation if the following elements are selected for the simulation

- A State Machine.
- A State Machine diagram.
- A class whose Classifier Behavior is defined by a State Machine.
- An InstanceSpecification whose Classifier is a Class that has a defined Classifier Behavior with a State Machine.

The function of Triggers is to change the State of a runtime object during a State Machine simulation. The Trigger can be either a signal or a time Event. If it is a signal Event Trigger, a signal will be sent to a runtime object to trigger it from one State to another. To send the Trigger signal, you have to select a runtime object, which is a target for the signal, in the **Variables** pane. All signals that can be received by the selected runtime object will be listed on the **Trigger** drop-down menu on the **Simulation** window toolbar as follows

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Trigger drop-down menu.

You can use a User Interface Mockup to send a signal to a runtime object. See more information about UI mockup in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space='MSI2024xR3'].

You can trigger a transition directly on a diagram. The menu **Fire Transition** on the smart manipulation toolbar triggers a transition and sends it to the activeObject that is displayed on the diagram. The **Fire Transition** command ignores guards.

To trigger a transition directly on a diagram, do either of the following

- Either select a target object from the Variables pane and right-click a transition line on the diagram, and select Fire Transition .
- During the simulation, click a transition line on the diagram and select Fire Transition .

123795413

**Related pages**

- State Machine
- State Machine diagram
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UI modeling diagram simulation' space='MSI2024xR3']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The Trigger can be either a signal or a time Event. If it is a signal Event Trigger, a signal will be sent to a runtime object to trigger it from one State to another. To send the Trigger signal, you have to select a runtime object, which is a target for the signal, in the <strong>Variables</strong> pane. All signals that can be received by the selected runtime object will be listed on the <strong>Trigger</strong> drop-down menu on the <strong>Simulation</strong> window toolbar as follows <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="The Triggers Drop-down Menu" ac:alt="The Triggers Drop-down Menu"><ri:attachment ri:filename="110250_RunState.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Running a State Machine simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />The Trigger drop-down menu.</h6><p>You can use a User Interface Mockup to send a signal to a runtime object. See more information about UI mockup in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UI modeling diagram simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[UI Modeling Diagram Simulation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p><p>You can trigger a transition directly on a diagram. The menu <strong>Fire Transition</strong> on the smart manipulation toolbar triggers a transition and sends it to the activeObject that is displayed on the diagram. The <strong>Fire Transition</strong> command ignores guards.</p><p>To trigger a transition directly on a diagram, do either of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>Either select a target object from the <strong>Variables</strong> pane and right-click a transition line on the diagram, and select <strong>Fire Transition</strong>. </li><li>During the simulation, click a transition line on the diagram and select <strong>Fire Transition</strong>.</li></ul><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4604861e-0862-412f-9815-995eac59591b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">123795413</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MSI2024xR3/State+Machine">State Machine</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR3/State+Machine+diagram">State Machine diagram</a></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UI modeling diagram simulation" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178217 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00192: Running server-side simulation with SSL

- page_id: `227178217`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178217/Running+server-side+simulation+with+SSL
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T09:22:08.300+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Server-side simulation > Simulation using Jupyter Notebook
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Prerequisites

INLINE

This chapter describes two ways to run server-side simulation with a self-signed certificate.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- The self-signed certificate used for server-side simulation must be generated for the IP (or hostname) of the machine where Web Application Platform is located. This is necessary because Web Application Platform (and not Teamwork Cloud) is called when connecting to the server. Therefore, if Teamwork Cloud is deployed on a different server, do not use the same certificate for both Teamwork Cloud and Web Application Platform.
- The certificate file must be in the .pem format.

To run server-side simulation with SSL

- Do one of the following:
  - Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform and provide the path to the certificate file: In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated. ', '', '', verifySSL='')]]>
  - Add the self-signed certificate to the Python trusted certificates list by completing the following steps:
    1. In Jupyter Notebook, execute the following commands to find the path, where the *cacert.pem* file of trusted certificates is located:
    2. Open the certificate file with a text editor and copy all information inside the file.
    3. Open the *cacert.pem* file with a text editor and paste the copied information at the end of the file, as shown below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
    4. Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform: In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated. ', '', '', verifySSL=True)]]>

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc215d10-bab6-4478-b725-1e1a7dbc1ece"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This chapter describes two ways to run server-side simulation with a self-signed certificate.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d10421a6-9839-4a9f-a8ad-00a71d06b036"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>The self-signed certificate used for server-side simulation must be generated for the IP (or hostname) of the machine where Web Application Platform is located. This is necessary because Web Application Platform (and not Teamwork Cloud) is called when connecting to the server. Therefore, if Teamwork Cloud is deployed on a different server, do not use the same certificate for both Teamwork Cloud and Web Application Platform.</li><li>The certificate file must be in the <em>.pem</em> format.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run server-side simulation with SSL</p><hr /><ul><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform and provide the path to the certificate file:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="770c82a9-1454-4d9e-b060-2e8228acee95"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c1bcaa5-4c7c-45c1-b16b-7c6951a4951d"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client = SimulationWebClient('<WebApp_server_IP_or_hostname>', '<user_name>', '<password>', verifySSL='<path_to_certificate_file>')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Add the self-signed certificate to the Python trusted certificates list by completing the following steps:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In Jupyter Notebook, execute the following commands to find the path, where the <em><span style="color: rgb(255,153,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">cacert.pem</span></span></em> file of trusted certificates is located:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44470736-2fb7-4956-8283-56c858d6f6c2"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import certifi
print(certifi.where())]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(255,153,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Open the certificate file with a text editor and copy all information inside the file.</span></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(255,153,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Open the <em>cacert.pem</em> file with a text editor and paste the copied information at the end of the file, as shown below.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="691" ac:width="1076"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cacert_file.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Running server-side simulation with SSL" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Use the following request to connect to Web Application Platform:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7d4904d8-d376-4cd3-ac5d-8ef42c2d3da0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the request, make sure to provide the IP or hostname for which the certificate was generated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f7fc4737-7060-482a-9c78-b7a224fe241e"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client = SimulationWebClient('WebApp_server_IP_or_hostname>', '<user_name>', '<password>', verifySSL=True)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

The topic describes about creating runtime objects and value in the Variables pane from Classifiers and Instance Specifications including automatic initialization, exporting runtime objects to InstanceSpecifications, and the Times series chart. This section also discusses about carrying values using connectors, checking values against feature types, and verification status of runtime values to be recorded.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178016 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00194: Sample project

- page_id: `227178016`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178016/Sample+project
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:29:51.365+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Parametric evaluator
- labels: ['parametric-simulation-samples']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Parametric Simulation sample projects are available in the *<*install_root*>/samples/simulation/Parametrics* directory. The SysML Parametric diagrams and InstanceSpecifications are as follows

- simple_parametrics.mdzip: basic Parametric Simulation.
- The CylinderPipe.mdzip sample: demonstrates how to deal with multiple values. It shows the calculation for the cost of raw materials that will be used to manufacture the cylinder pipes. It also demonstrates the use of OpaqueBehaviorAction to simulate the parametric.
- The ActParIntegrate.mdzip sample: demonstrates the use of OpaqueBehavior to simulate the parametric.
- SCARA manipulator.mdzip: demonstrate the use of Parametric Simulation to evaluate the position of end-effector of the SCARA manipulator from the given angles of actuators.
- MotionAnalysis.mdzip and SpringDisplacementUsingTimevariable.mdzip: show how to use Time Series Chart for plotting the runtime values.
- Trade-Study for Brayton Cycle.mdzip: this sample shows you how to perform a trade-study.
- Forward Contract Valuation.mdzip: a dynamic constraint usage in Parametric Simulation.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

All of the sample projects of the Parametric Simulation engine include a Simulation Configurations package that contains two SimulationConfig elements for normal and silent simulation. You can select this SimulationConfig class to start the Parametric Simulation engine.

**Related pages**

- Specifying the language for the expression
- Value binding
- Evaluating expressions
- Evaluation with causality
- Dynamic constraint
- Manual value updates using the Parametric Evaluator
- Communicating with evaluators through simulation console
- Built-in Math
- Integration with external Evaluators
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Trade study analysis' space='MSI2024xR3']
- Sample project

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The SysML Parametric diagrams and InstanceSpecifications are as follows</p><ul><li>simple_parametrics.mdzip: basic Parametric Simulation.</li><li>The CylinderPipe.mdzip sample: demonstrates how to deal with multiple values. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177608 space=MSI2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00195: Sample projects

- page_id: `227177608`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177608/Sample+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2020-12-29T08:24:02.116+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > State Machine simulation
- labels: ['sample-projects', 'state-machine-simulation-samples']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

103619828

103619839

103619826

**On this page**

44

103619829

The State Machine simulation sample projects are available in the built-in **Small Tests**samples, in *<modeling_tool_installation_directory>\samples\simulation\SmallTestSamples.mdzip* or from the Welcome screen under the **Simulation** group of the modeling tool. In this topic, the focus is on the **State Tests** section for the State Machine simulation sample projects as follows:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The State Tests section of the Small Tests samples.

- Do Activity in State This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Do Behavior in model execution. Run the **TestState_DoActivity** simulation configuration to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Do Activity in State sample.
- Guard This test is designed to demonstrate how to use Guards on Transitions in model execution. Run the **TestState_Guard** simulation configuration to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Guard sample.
- History This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Composite State with Shallow History and Deep History in model execution. Click the **Run** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The History sample.
- Regions This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Orthogonal State with Parallel Regions and Entry and Exit Activities in model execution. The Entry Activity is executed immediately after the State Machine is activated before any States in the inner regions. Next, all Initial States in the regions are activated simultaneously. The **Regions** sample demonstrates that multiple active States are executed at the same time. The Event list in the Simulation Console contains Triggers of all outgoing Transitions of all active States. If one of the parent States’ outgoing Transitions is triggered, the Exit Activity will be executed before the State Machine is deactivated. Click the **Run with Context** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Regions sample.
- Timing Events This test is designed to demonstrate how to use timing Events in model execution. Both relative time and absolute time are supported (**Is Relative=true/false**). Click the **Run** button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Timing Events sample.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Run the <strong>TestState_Guard</strong> simulation configuration to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Guard Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Guard sample.</h6></li><li><h4>History</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Composite State with Shallow History and Deep History in model execution. Click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="History Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The History sample.</h6></li><li><h4>Regions</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use the Orthogonal State with Parallel Regions and Entry and Exit Activities in model execution. The Entry Activity is executed immediately after the State Machine is activated before any States in the inner regions. Next, all Initial States in the regions are activated simultaneously. The Event list in the Simulation Console contains Triggers of all outgoing Transitions of all active States. Click the <strong>Run with Context</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Regions Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Regions sample.</h6></li><li><h4>Timing Events</h4><p>This test is designed to demonstrate how to use timing Events in model execution. Both relative time and absolute time are supported (<strong>Is Relative=true/false</strong>). Click the <strong>Run</strong> button on the diagram toolbar to see how it works.</p><h4 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Timing Events Sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h4><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Timing Events sample.</h6></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178219 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00196: Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook

- page_id: `227178219`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178219/Sample+projects+for+simulation+with+Jupyter+Notebook
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:31:10.982+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Server-side simulation > Simulation using Jupyter Notebook
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

Once you set up Jupyter Notebook, you can open and test sample projects created for server-side simulation using Jupyter. This chapter briefly describes the sample projects and explains how to execute them.

To access the sample projects

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulation using Jupyter Notebook' space='MSI2024xR3'] if you have not already done so.
2. Extract the < install_root >\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\pyST.zip file. The sample projects are located in the samples directory.

##### Bouncing Ball sample

The *Bouncing Ball* sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with FMU. In addition, you can draw a chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Add the Bouncing Ball sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the *Bouncing Ball* sample and draw a chart based on the CSV data

1. Run the *Bouncing Ball* project and get the CSV data. Request example for running the simulation
2. Draw a chart based on the CSV data. Request example for drawing a chart based on the CSV data

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *Bouncing Ball* chart drawn based on the CSV data.

##### Coffee Machine sample

The *Coffee Machine* sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- Add the Coffee Machine sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.
- To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Auto-generating HTML files' space='MSI2024xR3'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MD2024xR3'] .

To run the *Coffee Machine* sample and open the HTML UI window

1. Run the *Coffee Machine* project. Request example for running the simulation
2. Check the simulation status. Request example for getting the simulation status/simulation/api/ui/20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4/CoffeeMachine.html'],
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'config': 'Coffee Machine Web',
 'elapsedTime': 72742}]]>
3. Click the UI URL in the response to the status request to open the HTML UI window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The HTML UI window opened during the *Coffee Machine* project execution.

##### Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample

The *Spacecraft Mass Rollup* sample demonstrates server-side simulation with specified input and output parameters.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Add the Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the *Spacecraft Mass Rollup* sample with input and output parameters

1. Do one or both of the following:
  - Specify a set of input parameters with values to be provided for the simulation.
  - Specify a set of output parameters to be obtained after the simulation is complete. If no output parameters are specified, all initialized values are returned. Request example for running the simulation with input and output parameters
2. Get the simulation results. Request example for getting simulation results

##### Car Braking Analysis sample

The *Car Braking Analysis* sample project demonstrates the server-side simulation that calculates stopping distance. You can use this project for the following scenarios:

- Seeing the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.
- Calculating the stopping distance according to the car mass and speed.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Car Braking Analysis

To run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to see the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance

1. Run the *Car Braking Analysis* project while changing the mass of the car, e.g., by 20 kg. Request example for running the simulation with a changed mass
2. Draw a chart of the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance. Request example for drawing the chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The chart showing the relationship between the vehicle mass and stopping distance.

To run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to calculate the stopping distance

1. Run the *Car Braking Analysis* sample to calculate the stopping distance according to the specified car mass and speed. Request example for running the simulation to calculate the stopping distance
2. Draw a chart to show the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance. Request example for drawing the chart

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The chart showing the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.

##### Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis sample

The Hinge Monte Carlo sample project demonstrates a server-side simulation that runs the Monte Carlo analysis. In addition, you can draw a histogram chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisite

Prerequisite

Add the *Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis* sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.

To run the*Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis* and draw a histogram based on CSV data

1. Run the*Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis*and get the CSV data. Request example for running the simulation
2. Draw a histogrambased on the CSV data. Request example for drawing a histogram based on the CSV data

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The*Hinge Clearance Histogram*drawn based on the CSV data.

##### Cruise Control Widgets sample

The Cruise Control Widgets sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup that includes widgets. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.

#### NOTE: Prerequisite

Prerequisite

- Add the Cruise Control Widgets sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.
- To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Auto-generating HTML files' space='MSI2024xR3'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MD2024xR3'] .

To run the*Cruise Control Widgets*project and get the status of the specified simulation

1. Run the *Cruise Control Widgets*project. Request example for running the simulation
2. To get the status of the specified simulation. Request example for getting the status of the specified simulation/simulation/api/ui/160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559/SimulationWithWidgets.html'],
 'project': 'CruiseControl_Widgets',
 'config': 'Cruise Control with Widgets',
 'elapsedTime': 1657}]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The HTML UI window opened during the Cruise Control Widgets project execution.

**Sample projects**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='BouncingBall.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CoffeeMachine.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='SpacecraftMassRollup.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CarBrakingAnalysis.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.ipynb']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='CruiseControlWidgets.ipynb']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

This chapter briefly describes the sample projects and explains how to execute them.</p><p><br /></p><p>To access the sample projects</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Setting up Jupyter Notebook"><ri:page ri:content-title="Simulation using Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Set up Jupyter Notebook]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> if you have not already done so.</li><li>Extract the &lt;<em style="text-align: left;">install_root</em><em style="text-align: left;">&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.simulation\pyST.zip</em> file. The sample projects are located in the <em>samples</em> directory.</li></ol><h3>Bouncing Ball sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Bouncing Ball</em> sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with FMU. In addition, you can draw a chart based on the CSV data after the model execution. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f9e22db-5375-4835-9560-0e4f7ed34add"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the Bouncing Ball sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br />To run the <em>Bouncing Ball</em> sample and draw a chart based on the CSV data</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Bouncing Ball</em> project and get the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="450a45e1-0ee3-4711-9879-211e08462766"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.simulate('BouncingBall", config='Run BouncingBall')
csv = result['csvExports']['Bouncing Ball']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p>Draw a chart based on the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9f9f724e-cdb9-45d8-b14d-1f22da433201"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing a chart based on the CSV data</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80
x = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (0)) / 1000
y = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (1))
plt.xlabel('time (ms)')
plt.ylabel('height')
plt.title('Bouncing Ball', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.plot(x, y,)
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="bouncing_ball_chart.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;">The <em>Bouncing Ball</em> chart drawn based on the CSV data.</h6><h3><br />Coffee Machine sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e57371f4-34bc-43bc-b4a8-b4611cb39bd1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Add the <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</li><li>To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, <ac:link ac:anchor="Generating and attaching HTML files to Teamwork Cloud projects"><ri:page ri:content-title="Auto-generating HTML files" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[attach the HTML UI to the server project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[commit the changes to the server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Coffee Machine</em> sample and open the HTML UI window</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Coffee Machine</em> project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7920f543-c4e6-46c8-b203-dcca01e368b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.run('CoffeeMachine', config='Coffee Machine Web')

{'state': 'INITIALIZING',
 'simulationId': '20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4',
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'elapsedTime': 106}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p>Check the simulation status.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e1d6ea40-afbe-4c41-ab51-1e6e3f6ba55b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting the simulation status</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_status('20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4')

{'state': 'RUNNING',
 'simulationId': '20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4',
 'simulationTime': '0 ms',
 'ui': ['<server_address>/simulation/api/ui/20a84d37-0aba-4e12-9f14-ca381a5952f4/CoffeeMachine.html'],
 'project': 'CoffeeMachine',
 'config': 'Coffee Machine Web',
 'elapsedTime': 72742}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p>Click the UI URL in the response to the status request to open the HTML UI window.</p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="coffeemachine_UI.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The HTML UI window opened during the <em>Coffee Machine</em> project execution.</h6><h3><br />Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Spacecraft Mass Rollup</em> sample demonstrates server-side simulation with specified input and output parameters.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7be87388-c7c1-4983-b705-e7dd874544a3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the Spacecraft Mass Rollup sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Spacecraft Mass Rollup</em> sample with input and output parameters</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one or both of the following:<ul><li>Specify a set of input parameters with values to be provided for the simulation.</li><li><p>Specify a set of output parameters to be obtained after the simulation is complete. If no output parameters are specified, all initialized values are returned.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a94da246-c32d-4507-afc1-531f0b4ec0c2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation with input and output parameters</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[parameters = {
 "inputs":
   {
        "propulsion.thruster.me":32
        "telecom.amplifier.me":15
   },
 "outputs":
    [
        "me",
        "propulsion.me",
        "propulsion.tank.me",
        "propulsion.thruster.me",
        "telecom.me",
        "telecom.antenna.me",
        "telecom.amplifier.me"
    ]
}

client.run('SpacecraftMassRollup', config='spacecraft mass analysis', data=json.dumps(parameters))

{'state': 'INITIALIZING',
 'simulationId': 'fc5fa7eb-761a-4d31-9201-2f51f1754675',
 'project': 'SpacecraftMassRollup',
 'elapsedTime': 187}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><p>Get the simulation results.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a6b83af-2f64-47ca-9ab0-864be104dae4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting simulation results</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_result('fc5fa7eb-761a-4d31-9201-2f51f1754675')

{'outputs': {'me': 104.0,
  'telecom.me': 34.0,
  'telecom.amplifier.me': 15.0,
  'telecom.antenna.me': 19.0,
  'propulsion.me': 70.0,
  'propulsion.tank.me': 38.0
  'propulsion.thruster.me': 32.0},
 'verification': [{'property': 'propulsion.thruster.me',
   'status': 'fail',
   'value': 32.0,
   'requirements': [{'id': '1',
     'text': 'Estimated mass shall be less than allocated mass',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}],
   'constraints': [{'constraint': 'me < ma',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}]},
  {'property': 'telecom.amplifier.me',
   'status': 'fail',
   'value': 15.0,
   'requirements': [{'id': '1',
     'text': 'Estimated mass shall be less than allocated mass',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}],
   'constraints': [{'constraint': 'me < ma',
     'status': 'fail',
     'timestamp': 0}]}]}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><h3><br />Car Braking Analysis sample</h3><p style="text-align: left;">The <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample project demonstrates the server-side simulation that calculates stopping distance. You can use this project for the following scenarios:</p><ul><li>Seeing the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.</li><li>Calculating the stopping distance according to the car mass and speed.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76615562-f0b7-44b0-ad4a-e247f1be010d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>Add the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to see the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> project while changing the mass of the car, e.g., by 20 kg.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="47e7284f-7619-400e-8a1a-53a74d3362de"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation with a changed mass</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[mass = [0] * 10
distance = [0] * 10
for x in range(0, 5):
    mass[x] = 800 + 20 * x
    parameters = {
      "inputs":
      {
        "grossMass": mass[x]
      }
    }
    results = client.simulate('CarBrakingAnalysis_final', config='Vehicle Analysis no Matlab', commit_results=False, data=json.dumps(parameters))
    distance[x] = results['outputs']['stoppingDistance']
    print(mass[x], distance[x] )]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p>Draw a chart of the relationship between the vehicle mass and braking distance.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5688e939-e2c9-4553-9a42-f68b097a0744"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing the chart</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import numpty as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80)
plt.xlabel('mass (kg)')
plt.ylabel('stopping distance (m)')
plt.title('The relationship between vehicle mass and braking distance', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.plot(mass, distance)
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="vehicle_mass_vs_braking_distance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The chart showing the relationship between the vehicle mass and stopping distance.</h6><p><br /></p><p>To run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to calculate the stopping distance</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Run the <em>Car Braking Analysis</em> sample to calculate the stopping distance according to the specified car mass and speed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6fde3de-37a4-404f-93fe-f2717838d8ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation to calculate the stopping distance</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[speed = input("Enter the car speed (km/h):")
totalMass = input("Enter the car mass (kg):")

parameters = {
  "inputs":
  {
    "grossMass": totalMass,
    "speed": speed
  }
}

results = client.simulate('CarBakingAnalysis', config='Vehicle Analysis no Matlab', data=json.dumps(parameters))
distance = results['outputs']["stoppingDistance"]
requiredDistance = results['outputs']["requiredStoppingDistance"]
print("Stopping distance:", distance)
print("Failed requirements:")
print(json.dumps(results['verification'], indent=2))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p>Draw a chart to show the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f47443bd-7452-479b-b73b-9e4b91885b32"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing the chart</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

data = pd.DataFrame(
    {"distance": [distance]})
ax = data.plot(kind='bar'
              figsize=(10,7), color=['dodgerblue'], fontsize=13);
plt.axhline(y=requiredDistance, linewidth=3, linestyle='--', color='r')
ax.set_ylim([0, distance+50])
plt.title("car Braking Analysis Chart", fontsize=18, weight="bold")
plt.ylabel("Metre (m)", fontsize=15)
plt.text(0, distance+2, round(distance, 4), ha = 'center', fontsize=14)
ax.get_legend().remove()
plt.xticks(np.arange(1), ["Stopping distance"], rotation="horizontal", fontsize=15);]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="stopping_distance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The chart showing the car stopping distance with the threshold of the required maximum stopping distance.</h6><h3>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis sample</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The Hinge Monte Carlo sample project demonstrates a server-side simulation that runs the Monte Carlo analysis. In addition, you can draw a histogram chart based on the CSV data after the model execution.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="538933e4-c5a5-44a3-bfb3-8fd4bd9108bb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisite</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Add the <em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To run the </span><em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">and draw a histogram based on CSV data</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p style="text-align: left;">Run the<span> <em>Hinge Monte Carlo Analysis</em> </span>and get the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1528fd86-b448-4105-a45d-214bd41ee5ae"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.simulate('HingeMonteCarloAnalysis_Jupyter', config='Monte Carlo Analysis')
csv = result['csvExports']['clearance']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p style="text-align: left;">Draw a <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">histogram </span>based on the CSV data.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f79f78d3-b71f-406d-bcf9-821c40b3c5fc"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for drawing a histogram based on the CSV data</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.pyplot import figure
from io import StringIO

x = np.loadtxt(StringIO(csv), dtype='double', delimiter=',', skiprows = 1, usecols = (0))
numberOfBins = 30

figure(figsize=(16, 8), dpi=80)
plt.hist(x, bins = numberOfBins)
plt.xlabel('hinge.clearance')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.title('Hinge Clearance Histogram', fontweight="bold", fontsize=20)
plt.xticks(np.arange(min(x), max(x), 0.5))
plt.grid()
plt.show()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Histogram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;">The<span> </span><em>Hinge Clearance Histogram </em>drawn based on the CSV data.</h6><h3>Cruise Control Widgets sample</h3><p>The Cruise Control Widgets sample project demonstrates server-side simulation with a specified HTML UI mockup that includes widgets. With this project, you can open the HTML UI window during model execution.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d77c2833-7437-4b38-8402-4cfd760ba261"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisite</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Add the <em>Cruise Control Widgets</em> sample project to the Teamwork Cloud server.</li><li>To open the HTML UI during server-side simulation, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Auto-generating HTML files" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[attach the HTML UI to the server project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR3" ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[commit the changes to the server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To run the </span><em>Cruise Control Widgets </em>project <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">and get the status of the specified simulation</span></p><hr /><ol><li><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Run the <em>Cruise Control Widgets </em>project.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b1087c00-6f1a-4869-a881-08b239df94e0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for running the simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[result = client.run('CruiseControl_Widgets', config='Cruise Control with Widgets')
simID = result['simulationId']]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To get the status of the specified simulation.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="779e4536-7465-42a9-b731-8a53680197eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Request example for getting the status of the specified simulation</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[client.get_status(simID)

{'state': 'RUNNING',
 'simulationId': '160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559',
'simulationTime': '0 ms',
 'ui': ['<server_address>/simulation/api/ui/160e822a-5c22-4758-9f1d-d72a83351559/SimulationWithWidgets.html'],
 'project': 'CruiseControl_Widgets',
 'config': 'Cruise Control with Widgets',
 'elapsedTime': 1657}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1200"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cruise_Control_Sample.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>The HTML UI window opened during the Cruise Control Widgets project execution.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Sample projects</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="BouncingBall.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CoffeeMachine.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="SpacecraftMassRollup.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CarBrakingAnalysis.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="CruiseControlWidgets.ipynb"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample projects for simulation with Jupyter Notebook" ri:space-key="MSI2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227177106 space=MSI2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00197: Saving plot results to the model

- page_id: `227177106`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227177106/Saving+plot+results+to+the+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-06T14:31:06.003+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide > Simulation Configuration and UI modeling > Time Series Chart
- labels: ['plot-results', 'saving-plot-results']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1417988244

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1417988246

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1417988245

The parameter **Result Location** in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='MSI2024xR3']<<SimulationConfig>> allows you to record and save plot results on a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Time Series Chart' space='MSI2024xR3'] into the model when simulation is completed. You can save the plot results in the**Result Location**parameterin the following formats: PNG file, CSV, or HTML file by using the **Record Plot Data As** option in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR3'] of the Time series chart. The feature works with the Time series chart both stand-alone and in the UI diagram. You can then include the plot results into a generated document for reporting purposes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Saving plot results using resultLocation in the <<SimulationConfig>>.

To save plot results using **Result Location**

1. Open the Specification window of a <<SimulationConfig>> and set **Result Location** to an InstanceSpecification.
2. Open the Specification window of a TimeSeriesChart and set **Record Plot Data As** to the PNG, CSV, or HTML format.
3. **Run** the <<SimulationConfig>> until it terminates.****The expected plot result will be saved into the InstanceSpecification. You can review the result through specification of the InstanceSpecification.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- If Result Location is set to a package, the plot results will be saved into a new InstanceSpecification at each runtime. However, if Result Location is set to an InstanceSpecification, the results will be saved into the selected InstanceSpecification.
- After the simulation stopped, the plot results (both image and CSV values) will be saved in the Image and Comment properties of the specified InstanceSpecification if the Record Plot Data As option in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Time Series Chart' space='MSI2024xR3'] is not specified.

The following figure shows the plot image saved in the Specification of Instance Specification of the bouncingBall model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The plot image saved in the Image property of the specified InstanceSpecification of the bouncingBall model.

The image is too large to show in the **Image** property in the Specification window; therefore, you can verify it by dragging the InstanceSpecification into the diagram.

To verify a plot image saved in the Image property of the selected InstancedSpecification

1. Drag the InstanceSpecification to the diagram.
2. click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the top left of the InstanceSpecification. The **Compartments** shortcut menu will open. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Compartments menu icon on the top left of the InstanceSpecification. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select Stereotypes > Shape Image .

The following figure shows the CSV values saved in the Specification of Instance Specification of the bouncingBall model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The plot CSV values are saved in the Body of the Comment property of the specified InstanceSpecification of bouncingBall model.

You can also specify the **Record Plot Data As** option in the Time series chart to your preferred plot result format (PNG, CSV, or HTML). If PNG is selected, you can verify the saved plot image by dragging the InstanceSpecification to the diagram and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the top left of the InstanceSpecification to open the **Compartments** shortcut menu and select **Stereotypes** > **Shape Image**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Verifying the saved plot image of the InstanceSpecification via **Compartments** > **Stereotypes** > **Shape Image**.

If the **Record Plot Data As** option is HTML, the HTML source will be generated into the **Documentation/Hyperlinks** property group. You can customize the title, logo, font, and other layouts of the generated HTML source and use them in other reports.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### HTML generated into the Documentation/Hyperlinks field of the InstanceSpecification.

The generated HTML can be saved and opened with a browser as shown in the following figure

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The HTML files of the saved plot results (The Record Plot Data As option is HTML).

1417988243

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Time Series Chart' space='MSI2024xR3']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR3']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227178209 space=MSI2024xR3 version=10 -->
## PAGE 00198: Server-side simulation

- page_id: `227178209`
- space_key: `MSI2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MSI2024xR3/pages/227178209/Server-side+simulation
- version_number: 10
- version_date: `2025-07-31T12:54:05.690+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Model Analyst Documentation > User Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

If you work with Teamwork Cloud projects, you can simulate them on the server without using a modeling tool. You can execute your models using REST API, or the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud user interface (if there are Cameo Collaborator documents published from those models). You can also run your models using the Resources application and Python client (pyST.py).

The following demo shows the server-side simulation in action:

800450

##### System requirements

The following Web Application Platform system requirements apply to an environmentthat runs a single server-side simulation. If you set up an environment where you can run concurrent server-side simulations, the system requirements applicable for a single simulation must be adjusted accordingly.

Web Application Platform system requirements for the environment with server-side simulation:

- Simple non-iterative simulations:
  - 1-core CPU
  - 1GB of RAM
  - Sample projects: <install_root>\samples\simulation\SpacecraftMassRollup.mdzip <install_root>\samples\simulation\CoffeeMachine.mdzip
- Simple iterative simulations:
  - 2-core CPU
  - 1GB of RAM
  - Sample projects: <install_root>\samples\simulation\TradeStudyExamples.mdzip 
<install_root>\samples\simulation\ HingeMonteCarloAnalysis.mdzip
- Complex, time-consuming simulations:
  - 4-core CPU
  - 2GB of RAM
  - Sample project: [ATTACHMENT filename='ElectricityConsumptionAnalysis.mdzip']

##### Installation information

The server-side simulation service is one of many Web Application Platform services provided as a separate application. After successfully installing the server-side simulation service, the Simulation application is displayed on the welcome page of the Web Application Platform.Opening the application will redirect you to the Server-side Simulation Swagger page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Web Application Platform welcome screen

The server-side simulation service is installed automatically alongside Web Application Platform while using Teamwork Cloud or Magic Collaboration Studio installer. If you install Web Application Platform on a separate machine and use the Advanced Install option, select the server-side simulation service during installation. To learn more, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Web Application Platform Installation on a separate machine' space='TWCloud2024xR3'].

To install the Server-side simulation

1. Install Web Application Platform.
2. Open the < wap_*install_root*>\apache-tomcat\shared\conf \webappplatform.properties file and set the sim.license.checkout property to true .
3. Restart Web Application Platform.

##### Licensing information

The server-side simulation functionality only works with a floating Magic Model Analyst license managed by the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='DSLS installation and licensing' space='IL2024xR3']or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FlexNet license server installation and licensing' space='IL2024xR3'].

You can setup the license either via the webappplatform.propertiesfile or the Web Application Platform. You can also configure the remote FlexNet license server for Web Application Platform services. To learn more, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Advanced services configuration' space='MCS2024xR3'].

1. In the Web Application Platform, select **Settings>** **Simulation Toolkit Web license**. To learn more about Setting application, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Settings application' space='MCS2024xR3'] page.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click on the FlexNet tab, enter the license server name and click Apply . [ATTACHMENT filename='FlexNet.png']
  - Click on the DSLS tab, select the Organization defined or Custom radio button, enter the license server name and click Apply . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### NOTE: Concurrent simulations

Concurrent simulations

- If the license is set in the webappplatform.properties file, it cannot be configured via the Admin Console. To use the WebApp platform, remove the license entry from the properties file and restart the WebApp service.
- To be able to run concurrent simulations, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deployment example for Teamwork Cloud with services on Kubernetes' space='MCS2024xR3'] .
- When executing multiple simulations at the same time, every new concurrent simulation requires an additional Magic Model Analyst license.

##### Preparing projects for server-side simulation

To simulate a project on the server, it must meet the following criteria:

- A project has to be added to Teamwork Cloud.
- For simulation results to be saved, you need to specify the Result Location property of a Simulation Configuration.

To control the auto-starting and auto-terminating properties of server-side simulation, specify the**autoStart**and**autoTerminate**properties of SimulationConfig accordingly. To learn more, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SimulationConfig stereotype' space=''].

##### Required permissions

To perform server-side simulation, you need to have the following Teamwork Cloud permissions:

- Read Resources - allows you to simulate resources.
- Edit Resources - allows you to commit the simulation results to a resource.
- **Manage Simulations** - allows you to manage all simulations, including the ability to get and review simulation results and terminate simulations executed by other users.

To manage all simulations, in addition to the Manage Simulations permission, you need to have the Read Resources permission for the related resources.

##### Server-side simulation limitations

There are several limitations related to server-side simulation:

- Math engine integrations (Matlab, Mathematica, etc.) are not available.
- FMU/FMU co-simulation with Windows binaries is not available.
- UseCase Execution is not available.
- DSL stereotypes are not applied to created Slot and InstanceSpecification elements.
- Finding elements by Name (e.g Signals, Operations with ALH, mapping with ExcelHelper) is not available.

**Related pages**
